speaker: Unknown
en: This is 2014.

speaker: Unknown
en: That was the year that Slack actually launched.
zh: 那是 Slack 实际上发布的那一年。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was interviewed by MIT Technology Review and asked if we were working to improve Slack.
zh: 我接受了 MIT Technology Review 的采访，被问及我们是否在致力于改进 Slack。

speaker: Unknown
en: I said, "I feel like what we have right now is just a giant piece of shit.
zh: 我说，“我觉得我们现在拥有的东西就是一大坨狗屎。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just terrible and we should be humiliated that we offer this to the public."
zh: 它太糟糕了，我们把这种东西提供给公众，我们应该感到羞耻。”

speaker: Unknown
en: To me that was like, "You should be embarrassed."
zh: 对我来说，这意味着，“你应该感到尴尬。”

speaker: Unknown
en: If you can't see almost limitless opportunities to improve, then you shouldn't be designing the product.
zh: 如果你看不到几乎无限的改进机会，那么你不应该设计这个产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: Slack was famous for being one of the early, consumerized B2B SaaS products.
zh: Slack 作为早期的消费化 B2B SaaS 产品而闻名。

speaker: Unknown
en: At more than one company all hands, I made everyone in the company repeat this as a chant.
zh: 在不止一次的公司全员大会上，我让公司里的每个人都把它当作口号来重复。

speaker: Unknown
en: In the long run, the measure of our success will be the amount of value that we create for customers, and you can put effort into demonstrating that you have created this value and stuff like that, but there's no substitute for actually having created it.
zh: 从长远来看，衡量我们成功的标准将是我们为客户创造的价值量，你可以努力证明你已经创造了这种价值之类的事情，但实际创造出价值是没有任何东西可以替代的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something else I heard that you often espouse is friction in a product experience is actually often a good thing?
zh: 我经常听你宣扬的另一件事是，产品体验中的摩擦实际上往往是一件好事？

speaker: Unknown
en: It became an assumption that it should always be trying to remove friction when the challenge is really comprehension.
zh: 这已经成为一种假设，即它应该始终试图消除摩擦，而挑战实际上是理解。

speaker: Unknown
en: If your software stops me and asks me to make a decision and I don't really understand it, you make me feel stupid.
zh: 如果你的软件阻止我并要求我做出决定，而我真的不理解它，你会让我觉得自己很蠢。

speaker: Unknown
en: If people could get over the idea of reducing friction as a number of goal or reducing the number of clicks or taps to do something, and instead focus on how can I make this simple?
zh: 如果人们能够克服将减少摩擦作为一个目标或减少做某事的点击次数或轻触次数的想法，而是专注于我如何使这个变得简单？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do I prevent people from having to think in order to use my software?
zh: 我如何防止人们在使用我的软件时不得不思考？

speaker: Unknown
en: You started two companies, both famously pivoted.
zh: 你创立了两家公司，都以著名的转型闻名。

speaker: Unknown
en: I imagine many people come to you for advice on pivoting.
zh: 我想象许多人来找你寻求关于转型的建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: The decision is about have you exhausted the possibilities?
zh: 决策在于你是否已经耗尽了可能性？

speaker: Unknown
en: Creating the distance so that you can make an intellectual rational decision about it rather than an emotional decision is essential.
zh: 制造距离感，以便你能够对其做出理性的理智决定，而不是情绪化的决定，这是至关重要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the reason I say you have to be coldly rational about it is because it's fucking humiliating.
zh: 我说你必须对此保持冷酷理性的原因是因为这真他妈令人感到羞辱。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Stewart Butterfield, a founder and product legend who rarely does podcasts.
zh: 今天，我的嘉宾是 Stewart Butterfield，他是一位创始人兼产品传奇人物，很少做播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Stewart founded Flickr and then Slack, which he sold to Salesforce in one of the biggest acquisitions in tech history at the time.
zh: Stewart 创立了 Flickr，然后创立了 Slack，后来他将 Slack 卖给了 Salesforce，这是当时科技史上最大的收购案之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: There is so much product and leadership wisdom locked away in his head.
zh: 他的脑海里锁藏着如此多的产品和领导力智慧。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like our conversation just scratched the surface.
zh: 我觉得我们的谈话只是触及了皮毛。

speaker: Unknown
en: We chat about utility curves, something he calls the owner's delusion, a hilarious pattern he sees at companies he calls hyperrealistic work-like activities, what he's learned about product and craft and taste and Parkinson's law, why you need to obsess with not making your users think, the backstory on his legendary we don't sell saddles here memo, and so much more.
zh: 我们聊到了效用曲线，他称之为“所有者的妄想”的东西，他在公司中看到的一种有趣模式，他称之为“超逼真的类工作活动”，他关于产品、工艺、品味和帕金森定律所学到的知识，为什么你需要痴迷于不让你的用户思考，他关于他那著名的“我们不在这里卖马鞍”备忘录的背景故事，以及更多的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: A huge thank you to Noah Weiss, Chris Cordell, Ali Rael, and Johnny Rogers for suggesting topics and questions for this conversation.
zh: 非常感谢 Noah Weiss、Chris Cordell、Ali Rael 和 Johnny Rogers 为本次对话建议的主题和问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is a really special one and I really hope to have Stewart back to delve even deeper.
zh: 这是一个非常特别的节目，我真的希望 Stewart 能回来进行更深入的探讨。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you enjoy this podcast, don't forget to subscribe and follow it in your favorite podcasting app or YouTube.
zh: 如果你喜欢这个播客，不要忘记在你最喜欢的播客应用或 YouTube 中订阅并关注它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It helps tremendously.
zh: 这非常有帮助。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you become an annual subscriber of my newsletter, you get 17 incredible products for free for an entire year, including Devin, Lovable, Replit, Bolt, n8n, Linear, Superhuman, Descript, Wispr Flow, Gamma, Perplexity, Warp, Granola, Magic Patterns, Raycast, ChatPRD, and Mobbin.
zh: 如果你成为我通讯的年度订阅者，你将在一整年内免费获得 17 款令人难以置信的产品，包括 Devin、Lovable、Replit、Bolt、n8n、Linear、Superhuman、Descript、Wispr Flow、Gamma、Perplexity、Warp、Granola、Magic Patterns、Raycast、ChatPRD 和 Mobbin。

speaker: Unknown
en: Head on over to lennysnewsletter.com and click Product Pass.
zh: 请前往 lennysnewsletter.com 并点击 Product Pass。

speaker: Unknown
en: With that, I bring you Stewart Butterfield after a short word from our sponsors.
zh: 有了这个，在赞助商的简短插播之后，我为您带来 Stewart Butterfield。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's a puzzle for you.
zh: 这是一个给您的谜题。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do OpenAI, Cursor, Perplexity, Vercel, Plaid and hundreds of other winning companies have in common?
zh: OpenAI、Cursor、Perplexity、Vercel、Plaid 和数百家其他成功的公司有什么共同点？

speaker: Unknown
en: The answer is they're all powered by today's sponsor, WorkOS.
zh: 答案是它们都由今天的赞助商 WorkOS 提供支持。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're building software for enterprises, you've probably felt the pain of integrating single sign-on, SCIM, RBAC, audit logs, and other features required by big customers.
zh: 如果您正在为企业构建软件，您可能已经感受到了集成单点登录、SCIM、RBAC、审计日志以及大客户所需的其他功能的痛苦。

speaker: Unknown
en: WorkOS turns those deal blockers into drop-in APIs with a modern developer platform built specifically for B2B SaaS.
zh: WorkOS 通过一个专门为 B2B SaaS 构建的现代开发平台，将这些交易障碍变成插入式 API。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whether you're a seed-stage startup trying to land your first enterprise customer or a unicorn expanding globally, WorkOS is the fastest path to becoming enterprise-ready and unlocking growth.
zh: 无论您是试图获得第一个企业客户的种子阶段初创公司，还是正在全球扩张的独角兽企业，WorkOS 都是成为企业就绪并解锁增长的最快途径。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're essentially Stripe for enterprise features.
zh: 它们本质上是企业功能的 Stripe。

speaker: Unknown
en: Visit WorkOS.com to get started or just hit up their Slack support where they have real engineers in there who answer your questions superfast.
zh: 访问 WorkOS.com 开始使用，或者只需联系他们的 Slack 支持，那里有真正的工程师会非常快速地回答您的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: WorkOS allows you to build like the best with delightful APIs, comprehensive docs, and a smooth developer experience.
zh: WorkOS 允许您像最好的公司一样构建，拥有令人愉快的 API、全面的文档和流畅的开发者体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: Go to workos.com to make your app enterprise-ready today.
zh: 前往 workos.com 让您的应用今天就为企业做好准备。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by Metronome.
zh: 本节目由 Metronome 为您带来。

speaker: Unknown
en: You just launched your new shiny AI product.
zh: 您刚刚发布了您的新 shiny AI 产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: The new pricing page looks awesome, but behind it, last-minute glue code, messy spreadsheets, and running ad hoc queries to figure out what to bill.
zh: 新的定价页面看起来很棒，但在它背后，是临时的胶水代码、混乱的电子表格，以及运行临时查询来计算账单金额。

speaker: Unknown
en: Customers get invoices they can't understand.
zh: 客户收到他们无法理解的发票。

speaker: Unknown
en: Engineers are chasing billing bugs.
zh: 工程师们在忙于修复账单错误。

speaker: Unknown
en: Finance can't close the books.
zh: 财务部门无法结账。

speaker: Unknown
en: With Metronome, you hand it all off to the real-time billing infrastructure that just works.
zh: 有了 Metronome，您可以把这一切都交给实时计费基础设施，它就是能正常工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Reliable, flexible, and built to grow with you.
zh: 可靠、灵活，并且随着您的成长而构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: Metronome turns raw usage events into accurate invoices, gives customers bills they actually understand and keeps every team in sync in real-time.
zh: Metronome 将原始使用事件转化为准确的账单，给客户他们真正能理解的账单，并让每个团队实时保持同步。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whether you're launching usage-based pricing, managing enterprise contracts, or rolling out new AI services, Metronome does the heavy lifting so that you can focus on your product, not your billing.
zh: 无论您是在推出基于使用量的定价，管理企业合同，还是推出新的 AI 服务，Metronome 都承担繁重的工作，以便您可以专注于您的产品，而不是您的计费。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's why some of the fastest growing companies in the world like OpenAI and Anthropic run their billing on Metronome.
zh: 这就是为什么世界上一些增长最快的公司，如 OpenAI 和 Anthropic，都在 Metronome 上运行他们的计费。

speaker: Unknown
en: Visit metronome.com to learn more.
zh: 访问 metronome.com 了解更多信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's metronome.com.
zh: 那就是 metronome.com。

speaker: Unknown
en: Stewart, thank you so much for being here and welcome to the podcast.
zh: Stewart，非常感谢你来到这里，欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thank you for having me.
zh: 谢谢你的邀请。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm excited.
zh: 我很兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm even more excited.
zh: 我更兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm so honored to have you here.
zh: 我很荣幸你能来到这里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I never told you this, but you've been towards the very top of my wish list of guests I have on this podcast ever since I started this podcast a few years ago, so I'm very excited that we're finally making this happen.
zh: 我从来没告诉过你这件事，但自从几年前我开始这个播客以来，你一直在我最想邀请的嘉宾名单的前列，所以我们终于实现了这一点，我感到非常兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have so many questions for you.
zh: 我有很多问题要问你。

speaker: Unknown
en: My first question is just what the heck are you up to these days?
zh: 我的第一个问题仅仅是，你最近到底在忙什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like ever since you left Slack, we haven't heard much from Stewart.
zh: 我觉得自从你离开 Slack 以来，我们没怎么听到 Stewart 的消息。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm curious what you're up to you hopefully or just chilling.
zh: 我好奇你在做什么，希望只是在休息。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm mostly just chilling.
zh: 我大部分时间只是在休息。

speaker: Unknown
en: I left Salesforce two and a half years ago and I have a two and a half year old, so she was actually born three days after my last day, so a lot of time with family and it's an enormous privilege to be able to spend time with young kids while they're young.
zh: 我在两年半前离开了 Salesforce，我有一个两岁半的孩子，她实际上是在我最后一天之后三天出生的，所以有很多时间陪伴家人，能够在孩子还小的时候花时间陪伴他们，这是一种巨大的特权。

speaker: Unknown
en: No new company to announce or anything like that.
zh: 没有新公司要宣布或类似的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do get a lot of emails and texts.
zh: 我确实收到很多电子邮件和短信。

speaker: Unknown
en: Basically every three to six weeks there's this cycle because Cal Henderson who's the CTO of Slack and who also, we worked together on Flickr, so have worked together now for 23 years, have been talking about what we want to do next if there is something.
zh: 基本上每三到六周就有这样一个周期，因为 Slack 的 CTO Cal Henderson，我们也曾在 Flickr 上一起工作，所以现在已经一起工作了 23 年，一直在讨论如果有什么东西的话，我们接下来想做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: But honestly, the big challenge has been I think these things are destroying the world and what we're good at is making software.
zh: 但老实说，最大的挑战是，我认为这些东西正在摧毁世界，而我们擅长的是制造软件。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you find some way to make software that helped people use their phones less often, then that would be a big winner, but haven't come up with anything good.
zh: 所以如果你能找到某种方法制造软件，帮助人们更少地使用他们的手机，那将是一个巨大的成功，但还没有想出什么好主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of philanthropic work, nothing to announce there yet, but there's some cool projects that I'm working on, and a lot of just personal creative art projects and supporting other artists and stuff like that.
zh: 很多慈善工作，那里没什么可宣布的，但我正在做一些很酷的项目，还有很多只是个人的创意艺术项目和支持其他艺术家之类的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: To prep for this chat, I talked to so many people that have worked with you over the years to try to figure out what you taught them about building product, building teams, building companies that most stuck with them, that most helped them build amazing products.
zh: 为了准备这次聊天，我和这么多年来与你共事的人交谈过，试图找出你教给他们的关于构建产品、构建团队、构建公司的知识中，最让他们记忆犹新、最能帮助他们构建出色产品的部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: The first is a concept called utility curves.
zh: 第一个是一个叫做效用曲线的概念。

speaker: Unknown
en: This came up a bunch across so many people that have worked with you.
zh: 这在与你共事过的很多人中都被提到了很多次。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about what is a utility curve, how you use that to build better products.
zh: 谈谈什么是效用曲线，你如何用它来构建更好的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is pretty easy because it's a very familiar S-curve where you have, it's flat and it starts arcing up and then there's a really steep part and then it levels off again.
zh: 这很简单，因为它是一个非常熟悉的 S 型曲线，它是平坦的，然后开始向上弯曲，然后有一个非常陡峭的部分，然后再次变平。

speaker: Unknown
en: And on the horizontal axis, you can think of cost or effort and on the vertical axis, it's value or convenience.
zh: 在横轴上，你可以考虑成本或努力，在纵轴上，它是价值或便利性。

speaker: Unknown
en: It depends exactly what you're talking about, but the idea is the first bit of effort you put into something doesn't result in a huge amount of value.
zh: 这取决于你在谈论什么，但这个想法是，你投入到某件事中的第一部分努力不会产生巨大的价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's some magic threshold where it produces an enormous amount of value and then continued investment doesn't really pay off.
zh: 然后有一个神奇的阈值，它产生巨大的价值，但继续投资并没有真正的回报。

speaker: Unknown
en: The most basic example I can think of is let's say you're making a hammer, and on that bottom axis, it's now quality, and if the hammer has a handle that breaks with any impact, then is totally useless.
zh: 我能想到的最基本的例子是，假设你在制作一把锤子，在那个底轴上是质量，如果锤子的手柄一受冲击就断裂，那就完全没用了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you make it a little bit stronger, it's still pretty useless and it's like junk, junk, junk, junk, junk.
zh: 如果你让它稍微坚固一点，它仍然相当没用，就像垃圾，垃圾，垃圾，垃圾，垃圾。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, good, great.
zh: 好的，很好，很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then it doesn't matter anymore.
zh: 然后这就不再重要了。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're making an app, okay, this app's going to have users and so let's make a user's table and a database, and so far you have generated no value.
zh: 如果你正在制作一个应用程序，好吧，这个应用程序将有用户，所以让我们建立一个用户表和一个数据库，到目前为止你还没有产生任何价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: The reason I felt like this was so important is because we would talk about a feature, and usually features are thought of as a binary.
zh: 我觉得这很重要的原因是，我们会谈论一个功能，通常功能被认为是二元的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You either have this feature or you don't.
zh: 你要么有这个功能，要么没有。

speaker: Unknown
en: The argument I guess was have we just not invested enough in this or have we got all the value or convenience or quality or whatever that we could get out of this?
zh: 我猜争论是我们是否只是在这个方面投入不够，还是我们已经从中获得了所有的价值或便利性或质量或其他东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: And we had pointed diminishing returns and it just doesn't matter.
zh: 我们有递减的回报，这就不重要了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think in many cases, people will add a feature, it's not good enough and so people don't use it or appreciate it, but now you've added some complexity to the app and then people give up or take it back or they try something in testing and they don't get the results they want, and so they decide that this a thing is worth doing.
zh: 我认为在许多情况下，人们会添加一个功能，它不够好，所以人们不使用它或不欣赏它，但现在你已经给应用程序增加了一些复杂性，然后人们放弃或者收回它，或者他们在测试中尝试了一些东西，没有得到他们想要的结果，所以他们决定这件事值得做。

speaker: Unknown
en: We would try to really investigate and decide whether we were on the first shallow part of the curve, the second shallow part of the curve, or we're just coming up to it.
zh: 我们会尝试真正调查并决定我们是处于曲线的第一个平缓部分，曲线的第二个平缓部分，还是我们才刚刚接近它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think it's a lot easier to understand the value of this when you're talking about a specific app and a specific feature, but I think it was ultimately helpful in getting people to understand whether something was worth it or not.
zh: 所以我认为当你谈论一个特定的应用程序和特定的功能时，更容易理解这一点，但我认为这最终有助于人们理解某件事是否值得。

speaker: Unknown
en: So just to mirror back what I'm hearing, there's this, if you visualize this curve at the bottom, it's like I don't even know what this is.
zh: 所以为了回应我听到的内容，如果你在底部想象这条曲线，就像是我甚至不知道这是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then up the curve is like, okay, I sort of get it.
zh: 然后沿着曲线上升是，好的，我有点明白了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then at the top is, okay, I can't live without this now that I understand what this is for, it feels like it's a really a different way of thinking about getting to the aha moment for someone where they see, okay, saved items, I get it, I need to use this constantly.
zh: 然后在顶部是，好的，现在我明白了这个是做什么的，我不能没有它，感觉这是一种真正不同的方式来思考为某人达到顿悟时刻，他们看到，好的，已保存的项目，我明白了，我需要经常使用这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like this works both for a specific feature and also just for Slack, getting people to even understand here's what Slack can do for you.
zh: 这似乎对特定的功能有效，对 Slack 也是如此，让人们甚至理解 Slack 能为你做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then now I can't live without Slack.
zh: 然后现在我不能没有 Slack。

speaker: Unknown
en: And essentially this is a lens you use to figure out where to spend product resources because if you don't get up that curve to I get it and I can't live without it, nothing else matters.
zh: 本质上这是你用来确定在哪里投入产品资源的一个视角，因为如果你没有上升到那个曲线达到“我明白了，我不能没有它”的程度，其他什么都不重要了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is that the framework?
zh: 那是这个框架吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, and I think then you layer on another concept like the, Bezos used the term divine discontent.
zh: 是的，我想接着再说一个概念，贝佐斯曾用“神圣的不满”这个词。

speaker: Unknown
en: The line actually moves because once people are familiar with a piece of software or the way a feature is implemented or something like that, their standards go up, and so there's this competition.
zh: 基准线其实会移动，因为一旦人们熟悉了某款软件、某个功能的实现方式或是类似的东西，他们的标准就会提高，于是就有了这种竞争。

speaker: Unknown
en: And again, this axis can be, utility is the best general term for it, but it could be quality, convenience, speed, it could be any number of things, but as you improve your search capability or as you improve your login experience or your forget password experience or your checkout experience or whatever everyone else is as well.
zh: 同样，这个轴可以是——实用性是它最好的统称——但也可能是质量、便利性、速度，或者是任何数不清的东西，但当你改进你的搜索能力，或者当你改进你的登录体验、忘记密码体验、结账体验，或者随便什么体验时，其他人也都在这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there's this continued investment and when forget about thinking about a new feature, you're looking at how the product works overall and usually things get implemented once, and then if they're lucky, they get improved upon periodically.
zh: 所以这就有了持续投入，且不管思考新功能，当你审视产品整体运作方式时，通常东西只被实现一次，然后如果它们幸运的话，会定期得到改进。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most things get improved upon very infrequently and some things get improved upon never.
zh: 大多数东西得到改进的频率非常低，有些东西则从未得到改进。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to give an example at the absolute extreme because I actually don't know how long this has been, but I try not to criticize other people's software so much because I'm very familiar with the trade-offs and prioritization and how hard it can be and blah, blah, blah, blah.
zh: 我想举一个极端的例子，因为我其实不知道这问题存在多久了，但我尽量不这么严厉地批评别人的软件，因为我非常熟悉其中的权衡、优先级排序以及这事儿有多难，等等等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: But okay, so most people have the Gmail Calendar app on their phone.
zh: 但是好吧，大多数人的手机上都有 Gmail 日历应用。

speaker: Unknown
en: I travel a fair bit.
zh: 我经常出差。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm mostly in the Eastern Time Zone, sometimes in Mountain Time, sometimes in Pacific, sometimes in English time, and sometimes in Japan, Central Europe.
zh: 我大部分时间在东部时区，有时在山地时区，有时在太平洋时区，有时在英国时间，有时在日本、中欧。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's maybe 10 time zones, 12 time zones that I would ever choose.
zh: 我可能会选择大约 10 个、12 个时区。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you hit the option to set the time zone on an event in Google Calendar, on the iOS app, it presents all the time zones in the world in alphabetical order.
zh: 当你在 Google 日历的 iOS 应用中点击设置事件时区的选项时，它会按字母顺序展示世界上所有的时区。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I mean, there's probably worse orderings, but there's no value in that.
zh: 我的意思是，可能还有更糟糕的排序方式，但那样做毫无价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: And even when you start searching, it still presents them in alphabetical order by country with that turn.
zh: 即使当你开始搜索时，它仍然按国家字母顺序展示它们，带有那个转折。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if I'm in California and I'm trying to set the appointment for next week when I'm back in New York and I type in E-A-S-T and I get a bunch of garbage, okay, Eastern, and then the first one is Eastern Australia, New South Wales, and then Eastern Australia, Queensland, and then Eastern Australia, Daylight Savings and Eastern Australia standard time.
zh: 所以如果我在加州，试着为下周回到纽约时设置一个约会，我输入 E-A-S-T，然后得到一堆垃圾，好吧，Eastern，然后第一个是澳大利亚东部，新南威尔士，然后是澳大利亚东部，昆士兰，然后是澳大利亚东部夏令时，以及澳大利亚东部标准时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then you're like, "Well, fuck, I can't remember which one is Daylight Savings and which one is standard time?"
zh: 然后你会想，“好吧，见鬼，我不记得哪个是夏令时，哪个是标准时间了？”

speaker: Unknown
en: I could keep going like this for a while.
zh: 我可以像这样继续讲一会儿。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is an app that's used by at least hundreds of millions of people, presumably every single Google employee.
zh: 这是一个至少有数亿人使用的应用，推测每一位 Google 员工都在用。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's bananas how bad it is.
zh: 它糟糕得简直离谱。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's so many, there's all these clever things you could do.
zh: 有很多，所有这些聪明的办法你都可以做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like you know me, I'm on the West Coast, first option should be the East Coast and vice versa.
zh: 比如你知道我，我在西海岸，第一个选项应该是东海岸，反之亦然。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it definitely shouldn't be that every time zone is presented with equal value.
zh: 但绝对不应该是每个时区都以同等价值呈现。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't a couple hundred time zones.
zh: 我不需要好几百个时区。

speaker: Unknown
en: I grew up in Canada.
zh: 我在加拿大长大。

speaker: Unknown
en: Newfoundland has its own time zone, which is offset by half an hour.
zh: 纽芬兰有自己的时区，它偏移半小时。

speaker: Unknown
en: The population of Newfoundland has about half a million people.
zh: 纽芬兰的人口大约有五十万。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not that many people go to visit Newfoundland, maybe a million people in all of history so like a million and a half out of 8 billion people.
zh: 没有多少人去纽芬兰访问，也许历史上总共有百来万人，所以在 80 亿人中大约就是一百五十万。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's Newfoundland, the same with China time, which is like 25% of the world's population in this.
zh: 就有纽芬兰，中国时间也是一样，这里面占世界人口的 25%。

speaker: Unknown
en: Anyway, that was a little bit longer than I intended to go on this example, but it's crazy because no one's going to switch to Gmail or to G Suite, Google Calendar from Outlook Exchange because the time zone picker is good, so maybe in some sense it doesn't matter, but at the same time there's a real value in delighting customers and there's an emotional connection that they form or don't form.
zh: 无论如何，关于这个例子我想说的比我预想的稍微长了一点，但这太疯狂了，因为没人会因为时区选择器好用就从 Outlook Exchange 切换到 Gmail 或 G Suite Google 日历，所以可能在某种意义上这无关紧要，但同时也存在取悦客户的真正价值，以及他们建立或不建立的情感连接。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in some cases that could be really positive like they would recommend it.
zh: 在某些情况下，这可能是非常积极的，比如他们会推荐它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when they switch companies or decide to start their own company, they're going to choose to use this product or advocate for it because of that emotional connection and vice versa.
zh: 当他们换公司或决定自己创业时，他们会因为这种情感连接而选择使用该产品或为其辩护，反之亦然。

speaker: Unknown
en: They'll also be like, "I hate this thing that drives me bananas.
zh: 他们也会说，“我讨厌这东西，它快把我逼疯了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I really think we should stop using it," or advocate for the alternative.
zh: 我真的认为我们应该停止使用它，”或者为替代方案辩护。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think people just don't appreciate or come back to those things often enough.
zh: 我认为人们只是不够重视或不经常回头关注这些事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's this category of really essential parts of the app again like account creation, sign up, forgot password, things like that, that for most organizations very infrequently get a lot of love and iteration and improvement despite the fact that the quality bar has gone up across the board and continually goes up.
zh: 然后还有这一类真正的应用核心部分，比如创建账户、注册、忘记密码之类的事情，尽管质量标准全面提高并且持续提高，但对于大多数组织来说，这些事情很少得到大量的关注、迭代和改进。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's go down that rabbit hole a little bit more around delight and craft.
zh: 让我们再深入探索一下关于取悦和匠心这个兔子洞。

speaker: Unknown
en: Slack was famous for being one of the early, let's say consumerized B2B SaaS products.
zh: Slack 是最早实现所谓消费者化 B2B SaaS 产品的佼佼者之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: Slack leaned into delight and experience and craft and a great experience.
zh: Slack 专注于取悦、体验和匠心以及卓越的体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you just as a product leader, I'd say are known as very taste forward, very craft oriented leader, which is pretty rare and I think continues to be rare.
zh: 你作为一名产品领导者，我想可以说是以非常有品味、非常注重匠心而闻名的领导者，这非常罕见，我认为现在依然罕见。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's a few things I want to talk about here.
zh: 所以我想在这里谈几件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is taste.
zh: 一个是品味。

speaker: Unknown
en: I heard at a talk, you gave a talk on taste and you have a really unique perspective on just what taste is, what product taste looks like.
zh: 我在一次演讲中听说过，你发表过关于品味的演讲，你对品味是什么、产品品味看起来是什么样的有着非常独特的视角。

speaker: Unknown
en: Can you share that?
zh: 你能分享一下吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: There is a lot of you going back to the utility curves again, people who are obsessed with this one little thing and keep on adding more and more detailed improvements beyond the point where it makes much of a difference.
zh: 有很多你回到实用性曲线的情况，那些人沉迷于这一个小东西，并不断添加越来越多细节上的改进，超出了那能有多大影响的点。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I guess a couple of things about taste.
zh: 但我想关于品味有两点。

speaker: Unknown
en: So one is can you learn to develop it?
zh: 一个是你能学会培养它吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think so because the word literally comes from experiencing food and putting stuff in your mouth.
zh: 我认为是的，因为这个字面意思来自于品尝食物并把东西放进嘴里。

speaker: Unknown
en: And can people become better chefs with training?
zh: 人们能通过训练成为更好的厨师吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes, absolutely.
zh: 是的，当然可以。

speaker: Unknown
en: Undoubtedly, some people have a natural advantage and are born with this ability to make discernments that are difficult for other people to make and stuff like that.
zh: 毫无疑问，有些人生来就有天然优势，天生就有这种能力，能做出其他人很难做出的辨别，诸如此类。

speaker: Unknown
en: But you can definitely practice and you can definitely get better.
zh: 但你绝对可以练习，而且你绝对可以变得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: The second thing I'd say is you can create a real advantage for yourself, for your product, for your company by leaning into it because most people don't have good taste and don't invest.
zh: 我想说的第二件事是，你可以通过拥抱它来为自己、为你的产品、为你的公司创造真正的优势，因为大多数人没有良好的品味也不去投资。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're probably familiar with, again, Jeff Bezos line, your margin is my opportunity and pretty obvious what he meant by that.
zh: 你可能又熟悉 Jeff Bezos 的那句话，你的利润就是我的机会，他的意思很明显。

speaker: Unknown
en: I would tell the story at Slack over and over again.
zh: 我会在 Slack 一遍又一遍地讲这个故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: It actually made it part of the new hire welcome.
zh: 它实际上成为了新员工欢迎的一部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm in Vancouver at our Vancouver office and I'm going for a walk with Brandon Velestuk who's our, at the time creative director for product development, I think that was his title.
zh: 我在温哥华的我们的温哥华办公室，我和 Brandon Velestuk 去散步，他是我们当时的...产品开发创意总监，我想那是他的头衔。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we're in the Yaletown neighborhood in Vancouver so there's really narrow sidewalks because it used to be a warehouse district and now it's fancy restaurants and nail salons and boutiques and stuff.
zh: 我们在温哥华的 Yaletown 社区，所以人行道真的很窄，因为它以前是一个仓库区，现在则是高档餐厅、美甲沙龙和精品店之类的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as it does in Vancouver, it starts to rain.
zh: 就像温哥华常有的那样，开始下雨了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't have umbrellas.
zh: 我们没有雨伞。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're walking back to the office and most people have umbrellas and we're on these narrow sidewalks with people coming towards us with umbrellas.
zh: 我们走回办公室，大多数人都有雨伞，我们就在这些狭窄的人行道上，人们撑着雨伞迎面走来。

speaker: Unknown
en: We noticed how few people would move their umbrella out of the way.
zh: 我们注意到很少有人会把他们的雨伞挪开。

speaker: Unknown
en: And of course, the other person, their umbrella, the pokey bits are exactly at eye level for people walking towards them.
zh: 当然，另一个人，他们的雨伞，那些尖刺部分正好与朝他们走来的人视线齐平。

speaker: Unknown
en: We would get forced off the sidewalk or having to duck down or whatever.
zh: 我们会被挤下人行道，或者不得不低头弯腰，或者别的什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: It became a game like we were guessing is this person going to tilt their umbrella out of the way so we can pass or not?
zh: 这变成了一个游戏，就像我们在猜这个人会不会把雨伞倾斜让开以便我们通过？

speaker: Unknown
en: And something like one-third of the people would do it.
zh: 大约有三分之一的人会这么做。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we had this conversation about it where it's like, okay, I can think of three reasons why people wouldn't do it.
zh: 我们对此进行了讨论，就像，好吧，我可以想出三个人们为什么这么做的理由。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is they have very few avenues in their life to exercise power and this is one of them.
zh: 一是在他们的生活中很少有途径来行使权力，而这是其中之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're just, want to get out there and dominate people and cause suffering.
zh: 他们只是想出去统治别人并造成痛苦。

speaker: Unknown
en: Shouldn't ascribe to malice that which can be ascribed to ignorance so that probably is the explanation for a tiny, tiny, tiny percentage of people.
zh: 不应将能归因于无知的事情归因于恶意，所以这可能是极少数人的解释。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the other two explanations aren't that great either.
zh: 但另外两个解释也不那么好。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is that they see it's happening, they see they're pushing other people off the sidewalk or poking them in the eye or whatever, and they're just like, "Fuck, that's too bad.
zh: 一个是他们看到了这正在发生，他们看到他们把别人挤下人行道或戳到眼睛等等，他们就像，“该死，那太糟了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wish there was something I could do about that, but I can't think of anything."
zh: 我希望能对此做点什么，但我想不出任何办法。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And the last reason is they just don't notice it all.
zh: 最后一个理由是他们根本没注意到。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're just oblivious to their impact on other people.
zh: 他们对对他人的影响浑然不觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're so in their head, and I can't really think of any other explanations for it besides that.
zh: 他们太沉浸在自己的头脑中，除了那个我真的想不出任何其他解释。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we would say it's not like tilting your umbrella is our opportunity.
zh: 所以我们会说，不像倾斜你的雨伞是我们的机会。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's not a great rephrase of your margin is my opportunity, but your failure to really be consider it exercise this courtesy and really be empathic about other people's experience is an advantage that you can create a critical advantage.
zh: 那不是对“你的利润就是我的机会”的一个很好的改写，但你未能真正考虑它，行使这种礼貌，真正对他人经历感同身受，是你可以创造关键优势的一个优势。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that there's many reasons why Slack was successful at the moment.
zh: 我认为 Slack 在当时成功有很多原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was successful and we think we had a bunch of really wonderful tailwinds and all of that stuff, but it wouldn't have grown the way it did without those little conveniences which caused people to form an emotional connection because a lot of our growth came from startup A uses Slack, and then someone leaves startup A for startup B, and startup B doesn't use Slack yet.
zh: 它成功了，我们认为我们有一系列真正美好的顺风局，所有这些东西，但如果没有那些微小的便利，它就不会像那样增长，因为这些便利导致人们建立了情感联系，因为我们的很多增长来自初创公司 A 使用了 Slack，然后有人离开初创公司 A 去初创公司 B，而初创公司 B 还不使用 Slack。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they would be like, "Oh my God, you guys, you really, this is so good.
zh: 他们会像，“天哪，你们这些人，你们真的，这太好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We got to try it."
zh: 我们得试试。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And the spread was driven by that and people really genuinely advocating for it.
zh: 这种传播就是由这种驱动的，人们真的真心实意地提倡它。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is an amazing metaphor.
zh: 那是一个惊人的比喻。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that one moment became a value of product craftsmanship at Slack.
zh: 我喜欢那一个时刻成为了 Slack 产品工匠精神的一个价值观。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tilt your umbrella was a very common saying on company swag and stuff like that.
zh: “倾斜你的雨伞”是公司周边商品上非常常见的一句话之类的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there an example, I imagine there are many, but from the time of building Slack, especially in the early days where you chose to go big on craftsmanship and experience and delight versus speed where you thought looking back that was a really great idea and worth really core just to success.
zh: 有没有一个例子，我想有很多，但是从构建 Slack 的时候开始，特别是在早期，你选择在工匠精神、体验和愉悦感而不是速度上下重注，回想起来你觉得那真是一个好主意，并且非常值得成为成功核心。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's a bunch of little examples.
zh: 这里有一堆小例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Someone else came up with this idea, and I'm trying to remember who it was, but let's see, maybe Andrea Torres, maybe Ben Brown, something like that who was like, "Why did we ask people for email address and password if their ownership of the email address was the thing that allowed them to create the account in the first place?
zh: 别人提出了这个想法，我试着回忆是谁，但让我想想，也许是 Andrea Torres，也许是 Ben Brown，类似这样的人，他是说，“为什么我们要问人们要电子邮件地址和密码，如果他们对电子邮件地址的所有权是允许他们首先创建账户的东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why don't we just ask them for their email address and then send them a link?"
zh: 为什么我们不只问他们的电子邮件地址然后给他们发一个链接？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so when Slack's first version of the mobile app came out, we're like, "Typing your password on your phone if you have any minimal threshold of password hygiene is a terrible experience."
zh: 所以当 Slack 移动应用的第一个版本出来时，我们就像，“在手机上输入密码，如果你有任何最低限度的卫生密码门槛，那是一个糟糕的体验。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Capital H, lowercase Q, six, caret, period.
zh: 大写 H，小写 q，6，脱字符，句号。

speaker: Unknown
en: So let's just have them enter the email address.
zh: 所以让我们只让他们输入电子邮件地址。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'll send them a link.
zh: 我们会给他们发一个链接。

speaker: Unknown
en: The link will automatically open the app and authenticate them.
zh: 该链接将自动打开应用程序并对他们进行身份验证。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there's one, a little example.
zh: 所以有一个，一个小例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow.
zh: 哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you guys invented the magic link experience.
zh: 所以你们发明了魔法链接体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: Someone else invented.
zh: 别人发明的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to be clear that I had seen that idea somewhere else, someone else, a blog post about it or something like that.
zh: 我想明确一点，我在别处看到过那个想法，别人，一篇关于它的博客文章或类似的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: But we were the first ones, to my knowledge, that really scaled that and made it a standard.
zh: 但是我们是第一个，据我所知，真正扩展了它并使其成为标准的。

speaker: Unknown
en: There is another one which we really puzzled about in the very early days where people have a long history of using messaging apps from AOL Instant Messenger to SMS to WhatsApp, where their expectation is they get a notification for every message that's received.
zh: 还有另一个我们在早期真正感到困惑的事情，人们有使用消息应用程序的悠久历史，从 AOL Instant Messenger 到 SMS 再到 WhatsApp，他们的期望是每收到一条消息他们都会收到通知。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in the case of Slack, that doesn't make as much sense because you're a member of many channels and the messages may not be for you, and so that's why we have the @ tagging people.
zh: 而在 Slack 的案例中，这就不太合理了，因为你是许多频道的成员，消息可能不是给你的，这就是为什么我们有 @ 标记人的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we certainly didn't invent that, that was Twitter.
zh: 当然，我们并没有发明那个，那是 Twitter 发明的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But what we realized was people were signing up for Slack, and it's one engineer on this team inside of this larger organization, inside this larger company, and they would pull in the person next to them and they would say, "Let's try it out."
zh: 但我们意识到的是，人们在注册 Slack 时，在这个大组织内部的这个团队里只有一名工程师，他们会把旁边的人拉进来，说：“我们试试吧。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then they would send a message and then one person would be like, "I didn't get a notification.
zh: 然后他们会发送一条消息，然后有一个人会说：“我没有收到通知。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is bullshit."
zh: 这真是扯淡。”

speaker: Unknown
en: We reluctantly decided that we had to send notifications for every single message as the default for new accounts.
zh: 我们不情愿地决定，必须将每一条消息都发送通知作为新账户的默认设置。

speaker: Unknown
en: But once you had, I don't remember what the threshold would happen, I think it's once you had received 10 messages, we would pop up this little thing that says, "Hey, you have our default settings for notifications.
zh: 但是一旦你达到了——我不记得阈值是多少了，我想是一旦你收到了 10 条消息——我们会弹出一个小东西，说：“嘿，你使用了我们的默认通知设置。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't want Slack to be noisy for you.
zh: 我们不想让 Slack 对你来说很嘈杂。

speaker: Unknown
en: Would you like to switch to our-"
zh: 你想切换到我们的……”

speaker: Unknown
en: ... For notifications.
zh: ……通知设置。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't want Slack to be noisy for you.
zh: 我们不想让 Slack 对你来说很嘈杂。

speaker: Unknown
en: Would you like to switch to our recommended settings?
zh: 你想切换到我们的推荐设置吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And then they would just click a link and it would have what should be the default, which is, you only get a notification if it's a DM or someone tags you.
zh: 然后他们只需点击一个链接，就会变成原本应该是默认的样子，也就是，只有当它是私信或者有人标记你时，你才会收到通知。

speaker: Unknown
en: But we realized it was worth that investment to get people over the hump.
zh: 但我们意识到，为了让人们跨过这个门槛，这样的投入是值得的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'll give one more simple one and then one kind of more complex.
zh: 我再举一个简单的例子，然后再举一个稍微复杂一点的。

speaker: Unknown
en: One, people would just like the, I can't remember if it's called urgent or important, but the flag in Outlook, set the priority of a message for the recipients always got abused inside of every company.
zh: 第一，人们会喜欢那个——我不记得它叫紧急还是重要了——但是 Outlook 里的那个标记，为接收者设置消息优先级的标志，在每家公司内部总是被滥用。

speaker: Unknown
en: As soon as someone does it, everyone's like, "Okay, I'm going to do that too for my message."
zh: 一旦有人这么做了，每个人都会想：“好吧，我的消息也要这么做。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so all of your messages have the little flag and it becomes useless.
zh: 于是你所有的消息都有那个小旗帜，它就变得没用了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have @everyone, which causes a notification to be sent to every member of the channel when the message is sent and people would start, someone would find this feature inside of a organization.
zh: 我们有 @everyone（所有人），当消息发送时，它会导致向频道的每个成员发送通知，人们会开始——组织里的某个人会发现这个功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: They would @everyone, everyone would get a notification and then the next person to send a message who was like, " Well, my thing's more important than Bob's thing.
zh: 他们会 @everyone，每个人都会收到通知，然后下一个发送消息的人会想：“好吧，我的东西比 Bob 的东西更重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to also @everyone."
zh: 我也要 @everyone。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it became really obnoxious and people would complain about it, but it was, I don't know, I guess tragedy of the commons.
zh: 这变得非常烦人，人们会抱怨它，但这——我不知道，我想这是公地悲剧。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not quite exactly the same thing, but it was this real dynamic that happened over and over again.
zh: 虽然不完全是一回事，但这确实是一次又一次发生的真实动态。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we came up with what was called the shouty rooster, and internally we said, "Don't be a cock."
zh: 所以我们想出了所谓的“吵闹公鸡”，在内部我们说：“别做个混蛋。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But we didn't obviously say that publicly when you @everyone, a little rooster would pop up and it would have you sound waves coming out of its mouth and being really obnoxious and say, "Hey, this is going to cause a notification for 147 people in eight different time zones.
zh: 但我们显然不会公开那样说，当你 @everyone 时，会弹出一只小公鸡，它的嘴里会发出声波，非常烦人，并说：“嘿，这将会给分布在 8 个不同时区的 147 人发送通知。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you sure you want to send this message with the @everyone?"
zh: 你确定要用 @everyone 发送这条消息吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And of course, that worked amazingly and it dropped off.
zh: 当然，这效果惊人，这种滥用的行为就减少了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And again, it was really trying to shape people's behavior so that they used, one is not to be very flexible, but we knew that there was ways to use it that would be annoying and difficult for everyone.
zh: 同样，这真的是试图塑造人们的行为，让他们使用——一个是不太灵活，但我们知道有些使用方式对每个人来说都很烦人且困难。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so try to shape the communication culture inside the organization to take best advantage on it.
zh: 所以要尝试塑造组织内部的沟通文化，以便充分利用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: That feature still exists.
zh: 那个功能至今还在。

speaker: Unknown
en: I see that rooster all the, no, I don't see it all, well, actually I do @channel, because I run a big Slack, so I see that rooster, that survived.
zh: 我总是看到那只公鸡……不，我不总是看到……好吧，实际上我确实 @channel，因为我管理着一个大型 Slack，所以我看到那只公鸡，它幸存下来了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, that survived and good because it was a trivially easy thing to implement and made a really big difference.
zh: 是的，它幸存下来了，而且很好，因为这是一个实施起来微不足道简单的事情，但产生了巨大的影响。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it also taught people how the product worked, because people probably didn't know that @everyone or @channel... Didn't think about the cost, at least.
zh: 但它也教会了人们产品是如何工作的，因为人们可能不知道 @everyone 或者 @channel……至少没有考虑过代价。

speaker: Unknown
en: Genius.
zh: 天才。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's one more.
zh: 再举一个例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we decided we were going to Do Not Disturb as a feature.
zh: 所以我们要决定实施“请勿打扰”作为一个功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we had this, not conundrum, but you're trying to take into account all the different uses of Slack because by the time we implemented this, 2017, there was tens of thousands of paying customers, the organizations, hundreds of probably millions of users, maybe hundreds of thousands of organizations.
zh: 我们遇到了这个——不算难题，但你在试图考虑到 Slack 的所有不同用途，因为当我们实现这个功能时，2017 年，已经有成千上万的付费客户、组织，可能有数百万用户，也许是数十万个组织。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't remember how many.
zh: 我不记得有多少了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And everyone had set up stuff the way that they liked it, including things like ops alerts going into channels for on-call engineers for some of the biggest systems and apps in the world.
zh: 而且每个人都按照他们喜欢的方式设置了东西，包括将运维警报发送到频道，给世界上一些最大的系统和应用的待命工程师。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we couldn't just deploy it right away.
zh: 所以我们不能立即部署它。

speaker: Unknown
en: We realized that some of the decision-makers, the owners of the organizations were going to have really strong opinions about this.
zh: 我们意识到，一些决策者，组织的所有者，对此会有非常强烈的意见。

speaker: Unknown
en: We also realized that some of the end users are going to have strong opinions, and we wanted to figure out a way to balance the concerns and give people appropriate means of control.
zh: 我们也意识到，一些最终用户会有强烈的意见，我们想找到一种方法来平衡这些担忧，并给人们提供适当的控制手段。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we came up with this really elaborate system for the rollout, which was, we told everyone, I'm sorry, every Slack administrator that this was coming weeks before it came.
zh: 所以我们为发布想出了这个非常详尽的系统，也就是，我们提前几周通知了所有人——抱歉，是每一位 Slack 管理员——这个功能即将到来。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we told them that we were going to set a default for their organization, which I believe was either 7:00 P.M.
zh: 我们告诉他们，我们要为他们的组织设置一个默认值，我相信是晚上 7:00

speaker: Unknown
en: to 7:00 A.M.
zh: 到早上 7:00

speaker: Unknown
en: in their local time zone, or 8: 00 A.M.
zh: 他们的本地时区，或者是早上 8:00

speaker: Unknown
en: to 8:00 P.M., I can't remember which it was, but also that they could override that default, and also that the individual end users could override that system owner default.
zh: 到晚上 8:00，我不记得是哪个了，但也告诉他们可以覆盖那个默认值，而且个别的最终用户也可以覆盖那个系统所有者的默认值。

speaker: Unknown
en: And finally, that the system owner could, if they changed the default again, would override all of the end user's preferences and then the end users could override them again.
zh: 最后，如果系统所有者再次更改默认值，就会覆盖所有最终用户的偏好，然后最终用户可以再次覆盖它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it wasn't to create this dynamic where people were at war, but so that you could change a policy and then people could still customize and stuff like that.
zh: 这并不是为了创造一种人与人之间处于战争状态的动态，而是为了让你能够更改一项策略，然后人们仍然可以自定义以及做类似的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But this was a much longer and more convoluted process, but it allowed the millions of people who were using Slack to get the feature without creating a bunch of conflict and without people turning it off automatically.
zh: 但这是一个更长且更复杂的过程，但它允许数百万使用 Slack 的人能够获得该功能，而不会产生大量冲突，也不会让人们自动将其关闭。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think critically, with setting a bunch of defaults, because if we didn't set the default, most people wouldn't turn it on at all.
zh: 我认为关键在于，设置了一系列默认值，因为如果我们不设置默认值，大多数人根本不会开启它。

speaker: Unknown
en: If we didn't default you to Do Not Disturb from 8:00 P.M.
zh: 如果我们没有默认将你设置为晚上 8:00

speaker: Unknown
en: to 8:00 A.M.
zh: 到早上 8:00

speaker: Unknown
en: you probably, if you're the average person, wouldn't ever do it yourself.
zh: 的「请勿打扰」模式，

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's another elaborate example where I think that investment made sense because it was a critical feature for a lot of people.
zh: 你大概是，如果你是个普通人的话，永远不会自己去做这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if we hadn't done it that way, I think it would've caused a lot of complaints and conflict and stuff like that.
zh: 所以这是另一个详尽的例子，我认为那项投资是合理的，因为这对很多人来说是一个关键功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: Those are amazing examples.
zh: 而如果我们没有那样做，我认为那会导致很多抱怨和冲突以及类似的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I very much appreciate that Do Not Disturb feature when you guys launched that.
zh: 这些都是很棒的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: I still remember that coming out.
zh: 我非常感谢你们推出那个「请勿打扰」功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm sure a lot of people are very thankful for that.
zh: 我还记得它发布的时候。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 我相信很多人对此非常感激。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something else I heard that you often espouse, which is counterintuitive to a lot of people is about friction, friction in the product experience.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That friction is actually often a good thing.
zh: 我听到的另一个你经常提倡的观点，这对很多人来说是反直觉的，是关于摩擦，产品体验中的摩擦。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a feature, not a bug a lot of times if you use it well.
zh: 摩擦实际上往往是一件好事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about your experience there.
zh: 如果你善用它，它在很多时候是一个特性而不是一个缺陷。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 谈谈你的相关经验吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: So yes, and there's also another issue around friction, which is it became like a mantra or just kind of an assumption that you should always be trying to remove friction.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in some cases that's true.
zh: 所以是的，关于摩擦还有另一个问题，就是它变成了一种咒语或者一种假设，即你应该始终致力于消除摩擦。

speaker: Unknown
en: We would talk about it in Slack.
zh: 在某些情况下这确实如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was hard to market.
zh: 我们会在 Slack 中讨论这个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was hard to explain what it was if you had never used it before.
zh: 它很难营销。

speaker: Unknown
en: You could say a messaging app for businesses or whatever, but a critical disadvantage to Slack doing out-of-home advertising, putting up a billboard versus beer or cars is, no one needs to be explained why they would want a car or beer, but everyone will have to explain one day why they want Slack.
zh: 如果一个人以前从未使用过它，很难解释它是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the problem there is comprehension, and this will come up an enormous amount.
zh: 你可以说这是一个用于企业的消息应用程序或者别的什么，但是 Slack 进行户外广告投放、竖立广告牌与啤酒或汽车相比的一个关键劣势是，没有人需要被解释为什么他们会想要一辆车或啤酒，但每个人总有一天都必须解释为什么他们想要 Slack。

speaker: Unknown
en: So now imagine you want to get tickets to the Taylor Swift concert in San Francisco and you go to the Ticketmaster website.
zh: 所以这里的问题在于理解，这个问题会大量出现。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you think about both your comprehension, it's perfect to this case.
zh: 所以现在想象你想在旧金山购买泰勒·斯威夫特演唱会的门票，你去了 Ticketmaster 网站。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that translates into the specificity of your intent and the degree of your intent is also kind of maxed out.
zh: 如果你考虑到你的理解力，这对这个案例来说是完美的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So look, I really want to get these tickets.
zh: 这转化为你意图的明确性，以及你意图的程度也是某种程度上的最大化。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know exactly what they are.
zh: 所以听着，我真的很想买到这些票。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're Taylor Swift tickets for this date at this venue.
zh: 我确切地知道它们是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so in that scenario, it doesn't really matter if Ticketmaster's website is slow, it doesn't really matter if the payments page errors out, you're going to persist and get through it.
zh: 它们是这个日期在这个场地的泰勒·斯威夫特门票。

speaker: Unknown
en: So obviously they're better to reduce friction, but in some sense there's not a huge amount of value in doing that.
zh: 所以在这种情况下，Ticketmaster 的网站是否慢一点并不重要，支付页面是否报错也不重要，你会坚持并完成它。

speaker: Unknown
en: For most creators of products, there are a handful of cases where that really is true for you as well.
zh: 所以很明显，减少摩擦是更好的，但在某种意义上，这样做的价值并不是很大。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they include things like user registration, authentication, checkout flows for e-commerce.
zh: 对于大多数产品创造者来说，也有一些对你来说确实如此的情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: I am significantly more likely to buy something if there's Apple Pay or Shop Pay or something like that.
zh: 它们包括用户注册、身份验证、电子商务的结账流程之类的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm significantly less likely to carry through the purchase of something if I have to manually enter all of the fields of my address one at a time rather than having one of those address pickers.
zh: 如果有 Apple Pay 或 Shop Pay 或类似的东西，我购买东西的可能性会大大增加。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's crazy, but the issue is my intent isn't always 100%, and the specificity of my intent isn't always 100%.
zh: 如果我必须手动逐个输入我地址的所有字段，而不是使用那些地址选择器之一，我完成购买的可能性就会大大降低。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if your thing is direct to consumer T-shirts and you acquire customers through Instagram ads, all of them know what T-shirts are.
zh: 这很疯狂，但问题在于我的意图并不总是 100%，我意图的明确性也并不总是 100%。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "This looks like a good T-shirt to me."
zh: 所以如果你的产品是直接面向消费者的 T 恤，你通过 Instagram 广告获取客户，他们都知道什么是 T 恤。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I'm rarely 100% intent.
zh: 就像是，“这对我来说看起来像一件不错的 T 恤。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I might have a very specific intent, but my intent's like 70%.
zh: 但我很少是 100% 的意图。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you're, the amount of friction is above that, I'm not going to do it.
zh: 我可能有一个非常明确的意图，但我的意图大概只有 70%。

speaker: Unknown
en: But now, okay, people coming to Slack.com, some friend had mentioned Slack and talked their ear off at some point months ago, and then they saw a news article and then they saw someone's tweet and then they saw an ad on about the website they were visiting and they finally said, "Okay, I'm going to go to this website."
zh: 所以如果摩擦量高于那个值，我就不会去做了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So their intent is at the absolute minimum threshold, it was before that last event happened, they were below and now they're above, but they're just above.
zh: 但是现在，好吧，来到 Slack.com 的人，某个朋友在几个月前提到过 Slack 并且在他们耳边唠叨个不停，然后他们看到了一篇新闻文章，然后他们看到了某人的推文，然后他们又在访问的网站上看到了一个广告，最后他们说，“好吧，我要去这个网站。”

speaker: Unknown
en: The specificity of their intent like, "I need to get Taylor Swift concerts for this date at this venue."
zh: 所以他们的意图处于绝对最低的门槛之上，在最后一个事件发生之前他们是在下面的，现在他们在上面了，但他们只是刚刚在上面。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is also very low, because they're like, "It's a work thing.
zh: 他们意图的明确性，比如“我需要在这个日期在这个场地获得泰勒·斯威夫特的音乐会门票”。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm not sure it's a spreadsheet or a calendar or exactly what it is."
zh: 也是非常低的，因为他们像是，“这是一个工作用的东西。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So they were coming in at 0.1% over these critical thresholds.
zh: “我不确定它是一个电子表格还是一个日历或者确切是什么。”

speaker: Unknown
en: What was the challenge?
zh: 所以他们是以超过这些关键门槛 0.1% 的水平进来的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It wasn't friction, because it's not like they were aiming for something and they knew what they were aiming for and they were just trying to get themselves to that point.
zh: 那不是摩擦力，因为并不像是他们在瞄准某个目标，并且他们知道自己在瞄准什么，而且他们只是试图让自己达到那个点。

speaker: Unknown
en: What we had to worry about was creating comprehension and in two senses, what is this thing?
zh: 我们需要担心的是创造理解，在两个意义上，这是什么东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: And what am I supposed to do next?
zh: 我接下来应该做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And that creation of comprehension in the sense of explaining stuff, that creation of comprehension in the sense of the design of the UI, of the screen, of the page or whatever, and the visual hierarchy and the affordances that are there and the indication of things to interact with and which thing should be the next thing to do and all that stuff, that becomes really critical.
zh: 而那种在解释事物意义上的创造理解，那种在 UI 设计、屏幕、页面或其他任何东西的设计意义上的创造理解，以及那里的视觉层次和可供性，以及可交互事物的指示，以及哪件事应该是接下来要做的事，以及所有这些东西，变得非常关键。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think very, very few people recognize that.
zh: 我认为非常非常少的人意识到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're like, "I want to get people who come to my webpage to the sign up form as quickly as possible."
zh: 他们会说，“我想让访问我网页的人尽快到达注册表单。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But if they don't know what they're signing up for and they don't know what it's going to do after, is it going to spam them?
zh: 但如果他们不知道自己在注册什么，也不知道注册后它会做什么，它会给他们发垃圾邮件吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't know, "Am I going to have to pay on the next step or what?"
zh: 他们不知道，“我在下一步是否需要付费，或者怎么着？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Then they're just going to back out.
zh: 然后他们就会退缩。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this was a lifelong battle because the remove friction orientation is so deep in people.
zh: 这是一场终生的战斗，因为减少摩擦力的取向在人们心中太深了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, it really makes a difference in those cases where people do have an intent and they do know what they're trying to do is a poor approach when the challenge is really comprehension, and I think the secret is most, 70%, 80% or whatever of a product design is in that comprehension step because people, if they do ever open the preferences tab and look at all the options, rarely have an idea.
zh: 同样，在人们确实有意图并且确实知道自己在尝试做什么的那些情况下，它确实能产生区别，而当挑战真的是理解时，这是一种糟糕的方法，我认为秘诀是，产品设计的 70%，80% 或无论多少都在那个理解步骤，因为人们，如果他们确实打开了偏好标签页并查看所有选项，很少会有想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you can't teach them or make it possible for them to discover what the capabilities are, then they're not going to take advantage of them and they're not going to get as much out of it.
zh: 如果你不能教会他们或使他们能够发现功能是什么，那么他们就不会利用它们，也不会从中获得多少好处。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that the trick is for most of the unique parts of any application, most of the specific things that your app, your product, your software does are areas where the challenge is going to be comprehension inside of friction.
zh: 我认为诀窍在于，对于任何应用程序的大部分独特部分，你的应用程序、你的产品、你的软件所做的大部分具体事情，都是挑战在于理解而不是摩擦力的领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: It really could be anything Shopify, the purpose of the service for its end users is generally going to be kind to clear.
zh: 真的可以是任何东西，Shopify，对于其最终用户而言，该服务的目的通常会有点清晰。

speaker: Unknown
en: But most people, most first-time store openers don't know that they can get reports or if they know that they can get reports, they don't know what kinds of reports.
zh: 但是大多数人，大多数第一次开店的人不知道他们可以获取报告，或者如果他们知道可以获取报告，他们不知道什么样的报告。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if they know what kinds of reports they can get, they don't know how they can tweak them and what the timing should be and which things that are more important to display.
zh: 如果他们知道可以获取什么样的报告，他们不知道如何调整它们，时机应该是什么，以及哪些东西更重要需要显示。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I could go on and on and on and on, and people just don't recognize that.
zh: 我可以一直说下去，人们就是没有意识到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I want to see if this is still true.
zh: 所以我想看看这是否仍然成立。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm just going to open my phone and clock app.
zh: 我要打开我的手机和时钟应用程序。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they had the craziest description for alarms.
zh: 他们对闹钟有最疯狂的描述。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a little bit different, but people can look at their own phone.
zh: 有点不同，但人们可以看自己的手机。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I have, it says alarms and it says sleep and a vertical bar, wake up and says, no alarm, and a button that says change.
zh: 所以我有，它显示闹钟，它显示睡眠和一个竖条，唤醒，并显示，无闹钟，以及一个说更改的按钮。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then if you hit it, it says sleep is off.
zh: 然后如果你点击它，它显示睡眠已关闭。

speaker: Unknown
en: In order to automatically turn on sleep features and edit your schedule, you need to turn sleep on.
zh: 为了自动打开睡眠功能并编辑你的时间表，你需要打开睡眠。

speaker: Unknown
en: So obviously sleep was a good name for this thing if you already had a way of getting people to understand it.
zh: 所以显然，如果你已经有办法让人们理解它，那么睡眠是这件东西的好名字。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you don't, it's ungrammatical and incomprehensible and why would you ever do it?
zh: 如果没有，这是不合语法的和难以理解的，你为什么要那样做？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I got to guess, it's been like this for years, 90 plus percent and maybe 98% of people just do what I do, which is that you just create, "I want the alarm on and I'm going to set the time for it."
zh: 我敢猜，这已经很多年都是这样了，90% 以上甚至可能是 98% 的人只是做我做的事，那就是你只是创建，“我想要闹钟打开，我要为此设置时间。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't know what turning sleep on does, but it's just the lack of comprehension prevents people from getting the value.
zh: 我不知道打开睡眠是做什么的，但仅仅是缺乏理解阻碍了人们获得价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm sure that there's a bunch of value behind turning sleep on, whatever that means and people spend a lot of time on those features and it integrates with biometrics and your watch or who knows.
zh: 我确信打开睡眠背后有很多价值，无论那意味着什么，人们在这些功能上花了很多时间，它与生物特征识别和你的手表或谁知道的东西集成。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, I still don't know because turning sleep on is like, what does that do?
zh: 同样，我仍然不知道，因为打开睡眠就像，它是做什么的？

speaker: Unknown
en: And what is it going to cost me?
zh: 它会让我付出什么代价？

speaker: Unknown
en: And what impact it's going to have?
zh: 它会有什么影响？

speaker: Unknown
en: Those examples are just to me all over the place.
zh: 这些例子对我来说只是到处都是。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the reason I don't use most software where there was an actual choice point or the reason I don't use most features where there was a choice point for me is because I didn't understand what they were going to do and I don't give a shit.
zh: 我不使用大多数软件的原因，如果有一个实际的选择点，或者我不使用大多数功能的原因，如果对我来说有一个选择点，是因为我不理解它们要做什么，我也不在乎。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if there is one mantra that I would use to replace that it's, Don't Make Me Think, I don't know if you remember that book.
zh: 如果有一个咒语可以用来取代那个，那就是，别让我思考，我不知道你是否记得那本书。

speaker: Unknown
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And honestly, it's been many more than 10 years since I read it, so I don't even remember all of the examples in the book, but as a mantra that was up there with utility curves because for two reasons.
zh: 老实说，我读那本书已经超过 10 年了，所以我甚至不记得书里的所有例子，但作为一个咒语，它与效用曲线并列，原因有二。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is it's just like it's expensive to make a decision.
zh: 一个是做决定很昂贵。

speaker: Unknown
en: You literally burn glucose.
zh: 你真的会燃烧葡萄糖。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a metabolic action.
zh: 有一个代谢作用。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's ATP created in the mitochondria and your neurons and a bunch of stuff is happening and people do get decision fatigue and there is cognitive cost of all these things.
zh: 在线粒体中产生 ATP，在你的神经元中，一堆事情正在发生，人们确实会得决策疲劳，所有这些事情都有认知成本。

speaker: Unknown
en: But also there's an emotional aspect, which is, if your software stops me a second and asks me to make a decision and I don't really understand it, you make me feel stupid.
zh: 但也有一个情感方面，如果你的软件让我停顿一秒钟并要求我做出决定，而我真的不理解它，你会让我觉得自己很蠢。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm like, "I don't understand this."
zh: 我就像，“我不理解这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Some people, maybe their orientation is, "Okay, the software is stupid."
zh: 有些人，也许他们的取向是，“好吧，这个软件很蠢。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think most people are like, "Oh, I'm dumb."
zh: 但我认为大多数人是，“噢，我很蠢。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you ever talk to people who aren't especially technologically savvy, the canonical example is people who are under 50 talking to their parents about using some piece of software and what they're supposed to do, the parents always feel stupid like they're the ones that are wrong.
zh: 如果你曾经和不太精通技术的人交谈，典型的例子是 50 岁以下的人与他们的父母谈论使用某个软件以及他们应该做什么，父母总是觉得自己很蠢，好像是他们错了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if you're causing people to think, in the best case, it's unnecessary use of their biological resources, and in the worst case you've now made them feel bad, emotionally bad, and they're going to associate that with the product forever.
zh: 所以如果你让人们思考，最好的情况是，不必要地使用了他们的生物资源，最坏的情况是，你现在让他们感觉糟糕，情感上的糟糕，并且他们将永远把那种感觉与产品联系在一起。

speaker: Unknown
en: And these are things that are just kind of rolling one into the other.
zh: 这些事情只是有点混在一起。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I'm going to keep going with one last thing, because they just kind of come together, which is along with reduced friction, it's like reduce the number of clicks or taps it takes for someone to accomplish something which is almost always exactly the wrong thing.
zh: 所以我要继续做最后一件事，因为它们只是凑在一起，那就是除了减少摩擦力，就像减少某人完成某事所需的点击或轻点次数，但这几乎总是恰恰是错误的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the easiest way you could make any action in your app, a single click or tap by just exposing every single possibility on one screen that scrolls for thousands and thousands and thousands and thousands of pages.
zh: 这是你可以在你的应用中执行任何操作的最简单方式,只需在一个滚动数千数千数千数千页的屏幕上展示每一个可能性,就能实现一次点击或点击。

speaker: Unknown
en: And obviously that's terrible.
zh: 显然这很糟糕。

speaker: Unknown
en: So why do people think that a little bit of that is good?
zh: 那么为什么人们认为一点点那个是好的?

speaker: Unknown
en: And here's an example.
zh: 这里有个例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: You open a menu, there's 14 things that people might want to do.
zh: 你打开一个菜单,有 14 件人们可能想做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Level one is group them into like items and put a vertical, sorry, horizontal divider between them so at least people can kind of chunk and see what there is.
zh: 第 1 层是按类别将它们分组,并在它们之间放置一个垂直的,抱歉,水平分隔线,这样至少人们可以某种程度上分块并看到那里有什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Step two is present the two or three most common things or the five most common things, whatever and then have some form of other and then you go to a sub menu that has more items and the decision of how to tune that becomes incredibly important.
zh: 第 2 步是展示两三个或五个最常见的事情,不管是什么,然后要有某种形式的“其他”,然后你去一个有更多项目的子菜单,如何调整那个的决定变得极其重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to pick on Google again just because it is, I feel like I'm Donald Trump here, but I'm going to interrupt myself again with a story.
zh: 我又要拿 Google 举例子了,只是因为它是,我觉得我现在是唐纳德·特朗普了,但我要再次用一个故事打断我自己。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's-
zh: 它是——

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, let's do it.
zh: 是的,我们就这么做。

speaker: Unknown
en: At some conference or event, I don't remember what it was, and this is probably eight years ago and we're in the bar after the sessions ended at this thing.
zh: 在某个会议或活动上,我不记得那是什么了,这大概是八年前,在这个活动结束后我们在酒吧里。

speaker: Unknown
en: John Collison from Stripe is there and Sundar, CEO of Google is there.
zh: Stripe 的 John Collison 在那里,Google 的 CEO Sundar 也在那里。

speaker: Unknown
en: And John, sorry, Patrick goes up to Sundar and they can talk about anything.
zh: 然后 John,抱歉,Patrick 走向 Sundar,他们可以谈论任何事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Stripe wasn't the behemoth, it was now at that point, but it was still a significant company, was up and coming.
zh: Stripe 还不是那个巨头,它现在是那个了,但在那一点上它仍然是一家重要的公司,正在蒸蒸日上。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what does Patrick want to talk to the Sundar about?
zh: 那么 Patrick 想跟 Sundar 谈什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: It's in the Gmail app, the dragging of people.
zh: 是在 Gmail 应用里,拖动联系人。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you reply all to a message, you often want to change the two recipient to CC and move someone from CC to two or something like that.
zh: 当你回复全部一条消息时,你经常想把“收件人”更改为“抄送”并把某人从“抄送”移动到“收件人”或类似的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just how physically the degree of dexterity that's required to do that inside of the Gmail app is very high.
zh: 就是如何在 Gmail 应用内部在身体上做那件事所需的灵巧程度是非常高的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It still hasn't been fixed, but it really struck me that Patrick could have asked for anything.
zh: 它仍然没有被修复,但这真的让我感到震撼,Patrick 本来可以要求任何东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: It could have been any talk, it could have been a partnership.
zh: 它可以是任何谈话,它可以是一个合作伙伴关系。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was so irritating to him that it worked like this, he couldn't quite get over it.
zh: 它是如此让他恼火,它是这样工作的,他无法释怀。

speaker: Unknown
en: So anyway, back to bashing on Google, who in many respects do an incredible job and there's all kinds of amazing stuff they do on blah, blah, blah, but the Gmail actions on an individual email are broken into two very long menu items that are different.
zh: 所以无论如何,回到抨击 Google,在许多方面他们做了一项令人难以置信的工作,并且有各种各样他们做的令人惊叹的东西,等等,等等,等等,但是 Gmail 在单个邮件上的操作被分成了两个非常不同的长菜单项。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of them doesn't exist on either menu.
zh: 其中一个在任何一个菜单上都不存在。

speaker: Unknown
en: There is an unlabeled icon is the only way to do it, and that's to mark something as unread once it's read.
zh: 有一个未标记的图标是做这件事的唯一方法,那就是在已读后将某事标记为未读。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have no idea why some of the actions are in one menu and some of the actions are in another menu.
zh: 我不知道为什么一些操作在一个菜单里而一些操作在另一个菜单里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's because some of them have to do with an individual email and some of them have to do with the whole thread, but it doesn't seem very consistent.
zh: 我认为这是因为其中一些与单个邮件有关,而其中一些与整个线程有关,但这看起来不是很一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every possible thing is listed there in one place.
zh: 每一件可能的事情都列在一个地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it becomes incredibly difficult to use because sometimes you have to tap in both menus, read all of the options, and say, "Okay, I've used the process of elimination and it's not here, so it must be there."
zh: 所以它变得极其难以使用,因为有时你必须点击两个菜单,阅读所有选项,然后说,“好的,我已经使用了排除过程,它不在这里,所以它一定在那里。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Uber doesn't work like this anymore, but when I first brought this up to people inside of Slack, there was a moment when the Uber app, when you opened it was just, "Where would you like to go?"
zh: Uber 不再是这样工作的了,但当我第一次向 Slack 内部的人提出这一点时,有一个时刻 Uber 应用,当你打开它时只是,“你想去哪里?”

speaker: Unknown
en: And other.
zh: 和其他。

speaker: Unknown
en: And other was everything like change your payment method, set your location, anything you could do in Uber.
zh: 而其他就是一切,比如更改你的支付方式,设置你的位置,任何你在 Uber 里能做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was perfect because almost all the time people just wanted to choose where they wanted to go.
zh: 那是完美的,因为几乎所有时候人们只是想选择他们想去哪里。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes you wanted to change where your pickup was because you weren't there yet or whatever.
zh: 有时你想更改你的接送地点在哪里,因为你还没到那里或不管什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was just like, what could be simpler than, "I'm going to tell you where I want to go or I'm going to achieve something else."?
zh: 那就像,有什么能比“我要告诉你我想去哪里或者我要实现其他东西”更简单?

speaker: Unknown
en: I really tried to push people to what is the thing that people, or what is the two things or what is maybe three things that people could want to do here and then put everything behind other.
zh: 我真的试图推动人们去思考什么是人们可能想做的事情,或者什么是两件或也许是三件事情,然后把其他所有东西都放在“其他”后面。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then if it takes them eight clicks or taps to do something, but every single one is trivially easy, that's great.
zh: 然后如果做某事需要他们点击八次,但每一步都是微不足道地简单,那就很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you reduce that to two clicks or taps, but every part of it is this fraught decision where I'm opening all of the menus and trying to figure out which thing is the right thing, and the more, comparing three things to each other is this difficult four things, it's kind of geometrically more expensive to compare 15 different options all to the other to see if this is the one that you might want.
zh: 如果你将其减少到两次点击或点击,但其中的每一部分都是这个充满压力的决定,我在那里打开所有的菜单并试图弄清楚哪个东西是正确的那个东西,而且更多,比较三件东西是这件困难的四件东西,将 15 个不同的选项彼此比较以查看这是否是你可能想要的那个,在几何上更昂贵。

speaker: Unknown
en: That just becomes impossibly expensive.
zh: 那变得极其昂贵。

speaker: Unknown
en: So to me, those are all really connected.
zh: 所以对我来说,这些都是真正相连的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if people could get over the idea of reducing friction as the [inaudible 00:43:42] or reducing the number of clicks or taps to do something and instead focus on how can I make this simple?
zh: 如果人们能够克服减少摩擦作为那个[听不清 00:43:42]的想法或减少点击或点击的次数来做某事,而是专注于我如何让这个变简单?

speaker: Unknown
en: How do I prevent people from having to think in order to use my software?
zh: 我如何防止人们为了使用我的软件而不得不思考?

speaker: Unknown
en: How can I make this trivially easy?
zh: 我如何让这个变得微不足道地简单?

speaker: Unknown
en: One last example, because this was really influential for me.
zh: 最后一个例子,因为这对我真的很有影响力。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I was going back and forth in Vancouver in San Francisco at the time when we were talking about all this inside of Slack, and I was behind a teenager in line aboard the plane and it was like, we're on the jet way.
zh: 所以那时我们在温哥华和旧金山之间来回往返,当时我们在 Slack 内部谈论所有这些,我在飞机上排在一个青少年后面,那时我们在登机口上。

speaker: Unknown
en: It took a long time.
zh: 它花了很长时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was watching her use Snapchat and it was insane.
zh: 我在看着她使用 Snapchat,那太疯狂了。

speaker: Unknown
en: She was tapping at least four times a second, sometimes six or seven times a second.
zh: 她每秒至少点击四次,有时每秒六七次。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was like dismissing stories and doing stuff.
zh: 就像是正在划掉故事并在做事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But there was a fluidity to it because everything was like, do I want to see this again?
zh: 但其中有一种流畅性,因为一切都是像,我想再看一遍这个吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: Do I want to see the next story from this person?
zh: 我想看这个人的下一个故事吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: Do I want to switch to a different person?
zh: 我想要切换到另一个人吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Instead, a notification came up, she answered someone's thing, she took a selfie of herself and everything was just like... So she was tapping four times a second for six minutes.
zh: 相反，一条通知弹了出来，她回复了某人的消息，她给自己拍了张自拍，一切都就像……所以她在六秒钟内点击了四次。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, probably there was some breaks in there.
zh: 我的意思是，中间可能有一些休息。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was the highest and best use of Snapchat for a 15 year old girl in 2016 or whenever that was.
zh: 那就是 Snapchat 对一位 2016 年或不管那是哪年的 15 岁女孩来说的最高和最好的用途。

speaker: Unknown
en: And imagine if the goal was to try to make her tap less, how much of an impediment it would've been to the experience that both her and Snapchat wanted to create?
zh: 想象一下，如果目标是试图让她少点击几次，这对她和 Snapchat 想要创造的体验来说会是多大的阻碍？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so fun to listen to this and the examples you gave of, it gives us a lot of insight into the way your mind works of just constantly unsatisfied with the way other products work with your product.
zh: 听这个很有趣，你给出的例子让我们深入了解了你的思维方式，即对其他产品与你的产品协同工作的方式总是不满足。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's core.
zh: 我认为这是核心。

speaker: Unknown
en: Patrick is a good example of Stripe.
zh: Patrick 是 Stripe 的一个很好的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like that's a recurring theme with very successful product leaders is just constantly unsatisfied and unhappy with how things work.
zh: 我觉得这是非常成功的产品领导者的一个反复出现的主题，就是对事物的工作方式总是不满足和不高兴。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love just even the way you summarize this, just a really good reframing of, instead of obsessing with reducing friction and reducing steps, instead think, how do I reduce the amount of thinking the user has to do?
zh: 我甚至喜欢你总结这个的方式，这是一个非常好的重新构建，与其痴迷于减少摩擦和减少步骤，不如思考，我如何减少用户必须做的思考？

speaker: Unknown
