en: So we would hire 27 Royal product managers in Slack who immediately want to hire someone.
zh: 所以我们在 Slack 雇佣了 27 位 Royal 产品经理，他们立刻想雇佣某人。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, what the hell?
zh: 就像是，搞什么鬼？

speaker: Unknown
en: What would that person do?
zh: 那个人会做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And they articulate it this way, but essentially it's like, "Well, that person would do the product management and then I would do strategy."
zh: 他们会这样表达，但本质上就像是，“嗯，那个人会做产品管理工作，然后我就做战略。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Classic.
zh: 经典。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really, I think the essential thing to understand about this is it's not because people are evil, and it's not because they're stupid.
zh: 这真的，我认为要理解这件事的核心是，这不是因为人们邪恶，也不是因为他们愚蠢。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's to me, very related to everything is complex.
zh: 而对我来说，这与「一切都是复杂的」非常相关。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if maybe this is my butterfly's law, I haven't thought about this way before.
zh: 也许这是我的蝴蝶定律，我以前没这样想过。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I tweeted this a very, very long time ago like if you... Everything is simple if you have no idea what you're talking about.
zh: 但我在很久很久以前发过这条推文，如果你……如果你对正在谈论的事情一无所知，那么一切都是简单的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the other side of that is like if something seems simple, probably you don't understand it.
zh: 所以另一面就像是，如果某件事看起来简单，很可能你并不理解它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's obvious exceptions to that.
zh: 这方面有明显的例外。

speaker: Unknown
en: But for anything that involves a large organization or a lot of human beings, if the problem seems simple, you don't get it.
zh: 但对于任何涉及大型组织或很多人类的事情，如果问题看起来简单，那你就是没搞懂。

speaker: Unknown
en: So every budget process, no head of engineering know, head of sales, no CFO, no GC, who's ever going to come back and say, "Oh, I've actually think next year we can just hire fewer people.
zh: 所以每一个预算流程，没有工程主管，没有销售主管，没有 CFO，没有 GC，会回来说，“噢，我实际上认为明年我们可以只雇佣更少的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or we're going to keep it flat or we're going to shrink through attrition because we don't need any more people to do what we're doing."
zh: 或者我们要保持持平，或者我们要通过自然减员来缩编，因为我们不需要更多的人来做我们正在做的事。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Not because they're evil, not because they're stupid, but it's almost overpowering impulse inside the organization that often leads to disastrous results.
zh: 不是因为他们邪恶，不是因为他们愚蠢，而是组织内部几乎无法抗拒的冲动，往往会导致灾难性的结果。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there's an...
zh: 所以有一个……

speaker: Unknown
en: I'll give one example from Slack's history, and I have tried in the past to disguise this example so that no one feels bad about it but I... Unfortunately, the specifics are so important to the example that it's not disguised and so I'll just reiterate that the people involved aren't stupid or evil.
zh: 我会举一个来自 Slack 历史的例子，我过去曾试图伪装这个例子，这样没有人会对此感觉糟糕，但我……不幸的是，具体细节对例子来说太重要了，所以没法伪装，所以我重申一下，涉及的人不是愚蠢或邪恶的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one example that's from the outside.
zh: 还有一个来自外部的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the example inside of Slack was we introduced threads, which was the ability to reply to a message inside of a channel.
zh: 所以 Slack 里面的例子是我们引入了串式回复，也就是在频道内回复消息的功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: And let's say you, Lenny, post a message.
zh: 假设你，Lenny，发了一条消息。

speaker: Unknown
en: I, Stewart reply to it.
zh: 我，Stewart 回复了它。

speaker: Unknown
en: You will automatically get a notification.
zh: 你会自动收到一个通知。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now Sarah later on replies to the same message.
zh: 现在 Sarah 稍后回复了同一条消息。

speaker: Unknown
en: Both you and I, as people who have push in that thread will receive a notification that there's been more activity, and so on.
zh: 你和我作为在该串式回复中收到推送的人，都会收到关于有更多活动的通知，以此类推。

speaker: Unknown
en: So like every single time anyone replies to it.
zh: 所以基本上每当有人回复它时都是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when the feature first was released or like when we did the final product review before it was released, the input box was pre-populated with at the person before you in the thread.
zh: 所以当这个功能首次发布时，或者就像我们在发布前进行最终产品审查时，输入框会预先填充你在该串式回复中前一个人的 at 信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was using the feature and I would put the insertion point there, select all delete, and then start writing my message.
zh: 我在使用这个功能时，我会把插入点放在那里，全选删除，然后开始写我的消息。

speaker: Unknown
en: And even if I wanted to add someone specifically, I almost never wanted to start my sentence with at that, because it just made it hard to reference what they were saying before.
zh: 即使我想特别提及某人，我几乎从来不想在我的句子开头加 at，因为这使得很难引用他们之前所说的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I said, "Get rid of this because, A, I think most people won't use it.
zh: 所以我说，"去掉这个，因为，A，我认为大多数人不会使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or if they did want to add someone, they're not going to want to do it at the beginning of the sentence.
zh: 或者如果他们确实想提及某人，他们不会想在句子开头做这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the way, you're teaching them to use the product wrong.
zh: 顺便说一句，你们在教他们错误地使用产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because it's important that everyone understand that every previous poster in this thread will automatically receive a notification unless they've figured it."
zh: 因为重要的是每个人都理解，在这个串式回复中之前的每个发布者都会自动收到通知，除非他们退出了该串式回复。"

speaker: Unknown
en: So okay, we release it.
zh: 所以好吧，我们发布了它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Six months goes by and suddenly the at thing comes back.
zh: 六个月过去了，突然那个 at 的事情又回来了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I messaged someone around the team and I said, "Hey, there's been a regression.
zh: 所以我给团队里的某人发消息说，"嘿，这里出现了一个倒退。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is super weird.
zh: 这太奇怪了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know what happened.
zh: 我不知道发生了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the at thing came back."
zh: 但是那个 at 的事情回来了。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And they said, "Oh no, this is on purpose.
zh: 他们说，"不不，这是故意的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We did a bunch of research."
zh: 我们做了一系列研究。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I was like, "What?"
zh: 所以我就想，"什么？"

speaker: Unknown
en: And I went through this and it was, if I recall correctly, it wasn't even P-95 certainty on this analysis.
zh: 我仔细检查了一遍，如果我没记错的话，在这个分析中甚至没有达到 P-95 的置信度。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it was something like when we do this, threads are 2.17 messages long, versus 2.14 messages long on average for when we don't do it.
zh: 但是结果大概像这样：当我们这样做时，串式回复平均有 2.17 条消息长，而当我们不这样做时是 2.14 条消息长。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so first of all, why is a longer thread better?
zh: 所以首先，为什么更长的串式回复更好？

speaker: Unknown
en: Like maybe a shorter thread is better?
zh: 也许更短的串式回复更好？

speaker: Unknown
en: It can be fewer messages that people have to go back and forth.
zh: 这样人们来回的消息可能会更少。

speaker: Unknown
en: Also, that's such a tiny difference.
zh: 而且，这差别太小了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Also, again, I don't remember the actual statistical analysis, so I'm not going to claim that it was incorrect.
zh: 另外，同样，我不记得实际的统计分析，所以我不会声称它是不正确的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I'm pretty sure this was outside the bounds of certainty that they can have.
zh: 但我很确定这超出了他们能够拥有的确定性范围。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the real thing was, oh my God, so you guys put flags into the product, you A-B tested it.
zh: 但真正的问题是，天哪，所以你们在产品中加入了标记，你们做了 A-B 测试。

speaker: Unknown
en: You did the instrumentation.
zh: 你们做了埋点 instrumentation。

speaker: Unknown
en: You created tables in the database or whatever we're using to record all of that.
zh: 你们在数据库中创建了表或者不管我们用什么来记录所有这些。

speaker: Unknown
en: You wrote queries to pull that.
zh: 你们编写查询来提取数据。

speaker: Unknown
en: You created charts based on that data.
zh: 你们基于这些数据创建了图表。

speaker: Unknown
en: You had meetings to discuss it.
zh: 你们开会讨论它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just kind unpacking all of the things that would've had to happen for this to come back.
zh: 只是稍微拆解一下为了让它恢复必须发生的所有事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like thousands of person hours at a minimum, because any feature change at that scale of organization, it's involving like a dozen people.
zh: 这至少花费了数千人时，因为在那个组织规模的任何功能变更都涉及十几个人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Engineering, QA, analytics teams, project managers, user research and stuff like that.
zh: 工程、QA、分析团队、项目经理、用户研究和诸如此类。

speaker: Unknown
en: The problem with that, so I think it was a bad idea, right?
zh: 问题在于，所以我认为这是一个坏主意，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: But the problem with that was the difference that you could possibly achieve between having this feature and not having this feature is like this much whatever units you want.
zh: 但问题在于，拥有这个功能和没有这个功能之间可能实现的差别就这么多，无论你用什么单位。

speaker: Unknown
en: The cost of doing the analysis was this much.
zh: 做分析的成本是这么多。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's guaranteed to be a loser.
zh: 所以这肯定是个亏本买卖。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like there's just, there's no world in which anyone could imagine putting the at previous respondent in the thread at the beginning of the message could possibly make that much of a difference to the quality of Slack, and how much utility it provides for people and all of that.
zh: 就像根本没有世界能让任何人想象出，在串式回复中的消息开头放置 at 上一个回复者，可能会对 Slack 的质量产生如此大的影响，以及它为人们提供的所有效用等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: But you know that to put the feature flags in, to ship new versions of the product, to put the instrumentation in.
zh: 但你知道要加入功能标记，要发布产品的新版本，要加入埋点 instrumentation。

speaker: Unknown
en: To have it all the API calls to record every action that people take to do all the analytics, to create the dashboard.
zh: 要有所有的 API 调用来记录人们采取的每一个动作来做所有的分析，来创建仪表板。

speaker: Unknown
en: To put paste a screenshot of that into a Google Slides presentation.
zh: 要把它的截图粘贴到 Google Slides 演示文稿中。

speaker: Unknown
en: To send the invitations to the meeting, to reschedule the meeting because someone couldn't make it.
zh: 要发送会议邀请，要重新安排会议因为某人无法参加。

speaker: Unknown
en: To have everyone sit down and look at the thing.
zh: 要让每个人都坐下来看这个东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like guaranteed loser.
zh: 就像是注定输家。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I know that Fareed told you to ask me about this hyper realistic work-like activities.
zh: 我知道 Fareed 让你问我关于这种超写实工作状活动的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so here's my grand theory.
zh: 所以这是我的宏大理论。

speaker: Unknown
en: Hyper realistic work-like activities goes along with this other concept called known valuable work to do.
zh: 超写实工作状活动与另一个概念相伴而生，叫做已知的有价值工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when I say known, I mean both you know what it is and you know that it's valuable.
zh: 当我说已知时，我的意思是你既知道它是什么，也知道它是有价值的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the problem with almost every organization at the very beginning, you have an enormous amount of work that you know what to do, and you know that it's going to be valuable.
zh: 而且几乎所有组织在刚开始时的问题在于，你有大量的工作，你知道该做什么，也知道它会有价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: So like starting a business, open a bank account.
zh: 比如说创业，开个银行账户。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because there's almost infinite general value of opening a bank account.
zh: 因为开银行账户几乎有无限的通用价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to do it.
zh: 你必须得做。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very simple to do.
zh: 这非常简单。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so at the very beginning of any startup, they're like, "I'm creating a user's table, and I'm doing sorting passwords," and you're doing all the things that are kind of absolutely necessary.
zh: 所以在任何创业公司的最开始，他们就像，“我正在创建一个用户表，并且我正在做排序密码，”并且你正在做所有这些绝对必要的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And everyone knows exactly what they are.
zh: 每个人都确切地知道它们是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so everyone's going to work in the morning and they're like right on.
zh: 所以每个人早上上班时就像，太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I have 10 things to do, and every single one of them is something I know how to do.
zh: 我有 10 件事要做，每一件都是我知道怎么做的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's definitely going to be valuable.
zh: 而且这肯定会有价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: Time goes on.
zh: 时光流逝。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the relationship between the supply of work to do and the demand for doing work just starts to change.
zh: 待做工作的供给与做工作的需求之间的关系，就开始发生变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: More and more people get hired.
zh: 越来越多的人被雇用。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every product manager wants to hire a junior product manager.
zh: 每个产品经理都想雇一个初级产品经理。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every new person, the first person you bring in on the risk and compliance team is like, "Oh my God, there's so many risks and things we have to be compliant with.
zh: 每个新人，你在风险和合规团队招的第一个人的反应就像，“天哪，有这么多风险和我们需要合规的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: We better hire more people on my team to do more risk and compliance work."
zh: 我们最好在我团队里雇更多人来做更多的风险和合规工作。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Which probably to some degree is right.
zh: 这在某种程度上可能是对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But we're going to have more and more of those people and they're going to call meetings with each other.
zh: 但我们会越来越多地有这些人，而且他们会互相召集会议。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now suddenly you have all these people with work to do and you've done all the easy obvious stuff.
zh: 现在突然间你有这么多有工作做的人，而且你已经做完了所有简单明显的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now your questions are like, "God, should we do FedRAMP high and make a Slack version?
zh: 现在你的问题就像，“天哪，我们要不要做 FedRAMP high 并做一个 Slack 版本？

speaker: Unknown
en: Which is going to require us to have wholly separate physical infrastructure for the hardware that runs the software?
zh: 这将要求我们要有完全独立的物理基础设施来运行软件的硬件？

speaker: Unknown
en: And also a whole different operations team, which has only US citizens on it?
zh: 还要有一个完全不同的运营团队，里面只能有美国公民？

speaker: Unknown
en: What is the possible number of dollars that we could make from doing this?
zh: 我们做这件事能赚到的可能的美元数字是多少？

speaker: Unknown
en: And how much complexity is going to be when we want to do updates to the software because we update two totally separate independent systems and rec."
zh: 还有当我们想要更新软件时会有多少复杂性，因为我们更新两个完全独立独立的系统并 rec。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It just gets out of whack, and so people end up... Like if you hire 17 product marketers, you're going to have 17 product marketers worth of demand for work to do.
zh: 它就变得不协调了，所以人们最终……就像如果你雇了 17 个产品营销人员，你将会有相当于 17 个产品营销人员工作量需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you don't have sufficient supply of product marketing work to do, they're just going to do other stuff.
zh: 如果没有足够的产品营销工作供给，他们就会做其他事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, very important, not because they're stupid, not because they're evil.
zh: 同样，非常重要，不是因为他们蠢，不是因为他们坏。

speaker: Unknown
en: But because they're like, I'm a product marketer and I want to be recognized for my work.
zh: 而是因为他们就像，我是个产品营销人员，我想因我的工作而得到认可。

speaker: Unknown
en: And my spouse has criticized me because they take, I should have already got promoted in the last cycle, and they really got to demonstrate some wins here and whatever it is.
zh: 我的配偶批评了我因为他们拿了，我本该在上个周期就已经升职了，他们真的需要在这里展示一些胜利以及随便什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so people are like calling meetings with their colleagues to preview the deck that they're going to show in the big meeting to get feedback on whether they should improve some of the slides.
zh: 所以人们就像叫同事开会预览他们要在大会上要展示的幻灯片，以获得关于他们是否应该改进某些幻灯片的反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that hyper-realistic work-like activity is superficially identical to work.
zh: 而那种超写实工作状活动表面上与工作完全相同。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like we are sitting in a conference room and there's something being projected up there, and we're all talking about it.
zh: 就像我们要坐在会议室里，那里投射着一些东西，而且我们都在谈论它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's exactly what work is.
zh: 那正是工作的样子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Hopefully not all of work for everyone inside of your company.
zh: 希望不是你公司里每个人工作的全部。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that's exactly what we do when we're working.
zh: 但那正是我们在工作时所做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But this is actually a fake bit of work, and it's so subtle that I'll do it.
zh: 但这实际上是虚假的一点点工作，而且它是如此微妙以至于我会去做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Our board members will do it.
zh: 我们的董事会成员会做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every executive will do it.
zh: 每个高管会做。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the further you are from having all of the context and all of the information and the decision-making authority and stuff like that, the easier it is to get trapped in this stuff.
zh: 而且你越远离拥有所有的上下文和所有的信息以及决策权之类的东西，就越容易陷入这些东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And people will just perform enormous amounts of hyper-realistic work-like activities, and have no idea that that's what they're doing.
zh: 人们会只是执行大量的超写实工作状活动，并且完全不知道那就是他们正在做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the result of that, I guess, is that if you are a leader, if you're manager, director, an executive, you're the CEO, it's on you to ensure that there is sufficient supply of known valuable work to do.
zh: 我想那的结果是，如果你是一个领导者，如果你是经理、总监、高管，你是 CEO，这是你的责任去确保有足够的已知有价值工作供给。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there almost always is, but it's creating the clarity around that.
zh: 而且几乎总是有足够的，但这是在围绕那创造清晰度。

speaker: Unknown
en: Creating the alignment.
zh: 创造对齐。

speaker: Unknown
en: Making sure everyone understands it, but that's what they're supposed to be doing, and then obviously doing it.
zh: 确保每个人都理解它，但那是他们应该要做的事，然后显然还要去做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Amazing.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I could listen to Stewart's rants all day.
zh: 我可以听 Stewart 的咆哮一整天。

speaker: Unknown
en: Hyper- realistic work-life activities.
zh: 超逼真的工作生活活动。

speaker: Unknown
en: We need to coin this-
zh: 我们需要造这个词——

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
en: Unfortunately, it doesn't make a good acronym. It's pretty ugly.
zh: 不幸的是，它不能构成一个很好的缩写词。它相当难看。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
en: [inaudible 01:07:37].

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: [inaudible 01:07:37] it a try.

speaker: Unknown
en: And just to close the loop on that, the solution is the leader recognizing this is happening and stopping it.
zh: 为了结束这个话题，解决方案是领导者意识到这种情况正在发生并阻止它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Telling people why are we spending time on this thing that is not going to get us anywhere?
zh: 告诉人们为什么我们要把时间花在这个不会带我们去任何地方的事情上？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what you just said probably isn't the best way because that sounds like you're chiding them, and they're dumb.
zh: 你刚才说的话可能不是最好的方式，因为这听起来像你在斥责他们，而且他们很蠢。

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: Rather than merging and say like, "Hey, you guys are a bunch of idiots wasting your time on this thing that doesn't matter."
zh: 而不是合并然后说，“嘿，你们这群白痴正在这件无关紧要的事情上浪费时间。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Whose fault is it?
zh: 这是谁的错？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the manager's fault.
zh: 这是经理的错。

speaker: Unknown

zh: 这是任何人的 VP 的错。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the CX, whatever, it's the C... Ultimately, it's the leader of the organization that has the responsibility to make sure that there is sufficient known-valuable work to do.
zh: 这是 CX，不管怎样，这是 C…… 归根结底，是组织的领导者有责任确保有足够的已知价值的工作要做。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's actually harder than it might appear.
zh: 这实际上比看起来更难。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

en: Before we run out of time, I want to touch on two other topics.
zh: 在我们要没时间之前，我想讲另外两个话题。

en: One is, when people think of Stewart Butterfield, I think a lot of people think of, We Don't Sell Saddles Here.
zh: 一个是，当人们想到 Stewart Butterfield 时，我认为很多人会想到 We Don't Sell Saddles Here。

speaker: Unknown
en: Your legendary Medium post that is just, I don't know, it's become a historic piece of literature in the annals of product building and in startups.
zh: 你那篇传奇的 Medium 文章，我不知道，它已经成为产品建设和初创公司编年史中的历史文献。

speaker: Unknown
en: I haven't heard people ask you much about this recently.
zh: 我最近没怎么听人问你关于这个的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: So let me just ask a couple of questions.
zh: 所以让我问几个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: What was the reason you put that out?
zh: 你发布这个的初衷是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What was the backstory on writing that memo?
zh: 写那份备忘录的背景故事是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why was it necessary?
zh: 为什么它是必要的？

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
en: Well, it really was an internal memo.
zh: 嗯，它实际上是一份内部备忘录。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... Memo.

speaker: Unknown
en: Why was it necessary?
zh: 为什么它是必要的？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, it really was an internal memo and there's a bit of a digression.
zh: 嗯，它实际上是一份内部备忘录，这里有一点题外话。

en: One of the crappy things about Slack is if all your corporate communication is on email, depending on exactly how it works and what system you use, you probably walk away with an archive of everything you said at Company X. If it's Slack, once you're turned off, you lose access to all that history.
zh: 关于 Slack 的一件糟糕事情是，如果你所有的公司沟通都在电子邮件上，取决于它是如何工作的以及你使用什么系统，你可能会带着你在 X 公司说过的所有内容的存档离开。如果是 Slack，一旦你被关闭，你就失去了访问所有这些历史的权限。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it's kind of like, "Oh, man.
zh: 所以这有点像，“噢，伙计。

speaker: Unknown
en: If I had only exported all of my messages before I left, I would have all this stuff," but that was absolutely verbatim.
zh: 如果我离开前导出了我所有的消息，我就会有所有这些东西，”但这绝对是逐字逐句的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I did not change a word of what I said inside the company.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe at most 10, but I think it was eight people.

speaker: Unknown
en: It was before Slack launched even.

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, it was before Slack launched.
zh: 是的，是在 Slack 发布之前。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was when we're doing private beta.
zh: 那是在我们做私人测试版的时候。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the point of it was to start to instill those ideas as early as possible and really create this alignment inside of that small team so that it could persist to survive as we grew and scaled.
zh: 它的目的是尽早开始灌输这些想法，并真正在那个小团队内建立这种一致性，以便它能够在我们发展和扩大规模时持续存在。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, that was the idea.
zh: 是的，这就是想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the gist, just for people that aren't super familiar with it, but we'll link to it, is just it's not enough just to build a great product.
zh: 要点，对于那些不太熟悉它的人来说，但我们会链接到它，仅仅是建立一个伟大的产品是不够的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You just as much have to put effort into communicating what this does for them, the problem this is solving for them, the outcome this is going to achieve for them.
zh: 你必须同样努力地沟通这为他们做了什么，这为他们解决了什么问题，这将为他们实现什么结果。

speaker: Unknown

zh: 这是一个很好的思考方式吗？

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

zh: 同样，把它比作啤酒或汽车，啤酒可以追溯到史前文明。

speaker: Unknown
en: Cars were obviously [inaudible 01:10:38], but at some point you had to convince people why they would want a car instead of a horse.
zh: 汽车显然是[听不清 01:10:38]，但在某个时候你必须说服人们为什么他们想要一辆车而不是一匹马。

speaker: Unknown
en: For your new AI-based recruiting tool or your calendar app or whatever, there's some reason why you think that people should use yours instead of the thing that they're using now, which might be a wholesale one-for- one replacement, or more often is a change in the way that you're working that has a bunch of other adjacencies and you want to expand into these other categories.
zh: 对于你新的基于人工智能的招聘工具或你的日历应用程序或任何东西，有一些理由你认为人们应该使用你的而不是他们现在正在使用的，这可能是一个完全的一对一替换，或者更多时候是你工作方式的改变，它有一堆其他的邻接，你想要扩展到这些其他类别。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're not just responsible for creating the product, but also, to a certain degree, creating the market.
zh: 你不仅负责创造产品，而且在一定程度上还负责创造市场。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's this book, Positioning, which is an absolute classic.
zh: 有一本书叫《定位》，它绝对是个经典。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very short.
zh: 这本书很短。

speaker: Unknown
en: I would recommend everyone read it, where the point of it is, from my perspective, it's almost impossible to create a new idea in someone's head.
zh: 我建议每个人都读一读，在我看来，它的要点是，在某人头脑中创造一个新想法几乎是不可能的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's much easier to take a couple of existing ideas and put them together.
zh: 取几个现有的想法并把它们放在一起要容易得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's much easier to say it's like Jaws meets Star Wars, or it's Uber for Pets or something like that, than to come up with an actual new idea.
zh: 所以，说它像是《大白鲨》遇上《星球大战》，或者它是宠物的 Uber 或类似的东西，比提出一个真正的新想法要容易得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: But you have to do that because if your thing is different in any significant way from the alternatives, you're not just creating the product.
zh: 但你必须这样做，因为如果你的东西在任何重要方面与替代品不同，你不仅仅是在创造产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're creating the market.
zh: 你正在创造市场。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're really kind of one and the same.
zh: 它们实际上是一回事。

speaker: Unknown
en: The reason I wanted to touch on it is I think still people continue to not listen to this advice and continue to over-invest in more features, more products, things like that.
zh: 我想提到这一点的原因是，我认为人们仍然不听从这个建议，继续在更多功能、更多产品等方面过度投资。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just the specific example of, "We don't sell saddles here," just to quickly communicate this to folks, and correct me if I'm missing anything, is just instead of, "Hey, look at this amazing saddle we've bought," which you want to communicate as, "Here, go horseback riding.
zh: “我们这里不卖马鞍”这个具体例子，只是为了快速向大家传达这一点，如果我有什么遗漏请纠正我，不是说，“嘿，看看我们买的这个惊人的马鞍”，而是你想传达的是，“来吧，去骑马。

speaker: Unknown
en: Look at this incredible experience you can have."
zh: 看看你可以拥有的这种令人难以置信的体验。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then they decide, "Oh, shit.
zh: 然后他们决定，“噢，该死。

speaker: Unknown
en: I need to go buy a saddle to do that."
zh: 我需要去买个马鞍才能做到这一点。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And 100%, that aspect of it is not original because I think that's something that marketers have done for a long time, certainly in the marcom and advertising.
zh: 而且 100%，这方面并不是原创的，因为我认为这是营销人员长期以来一直在做的事情，当然是在营销传播和广告中。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you want to sell Harley-Davidson's, there are people who are going to geek out on the engines and stuff like that and the quality of the leather and stuff like that.
zh: 如果你想卖 Harley-Davidson，会有那些对发动机和皮质质量之类的东西着迷的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: But when you're selling the motorcycle, you're selling the open road and freedom and the wind in your hair.
zh: 但当你销售摩托车时，你是在推销开阔的道路、自由和风吹过头发的感觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you're Lululemon, you are obviously selling yoga pants, but you're also selling health and aspiration and being the best version of yourself and a bunch of other stuff.
zh: 如果你是 Lululemon，你显然是在卖瑜伽裤，但你也在推销健康、抱负、成为最好的自己以及其他一些东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh my God, I forgot the classic version of it.
zh: 天哪，我忘了那个经典版本。

speaker: Lenny
en: There's the ship ...
zh: 有那个关于船的……

speaker: Unknown
en: You're selling the screwdriver.
zh: 你在卖螺丝刀。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, yeah.
zh: 噢，是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The nail.
zh: 钉子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, the nail.
zh: 是的，钉子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Anyway.
zh: 无论如何。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, we missed that one.
zh: 是的，我们错过了那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, there's the one I think about is instead of trying to convince men to build a ship, instill a yearning for the sea.
zh: 嗯，我想到的一个是，与其试图说服人们去造船，不如灌输对大海的渴望。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Exactly.
zh: 确切地说。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's something that goes back in history.
zh: 这是历史上就有的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me ask you about pivoting.
zh: 让我问问你关于转型的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: You are potentially the king of pivots.
zh: 你可能是转型之王。

speaker: Unknown
en: You started two companies both famously pivoted, both from video games, which is why I asked you about that at the beginning, into very successful companies.
zh: 你创办了两家公司都很有名地进行了转型，都是从视频游戏开始的，这也是我在一开始问你这个问题的原因，变成了非常成功的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: I imagine many people come to you for advice on pivoting.
zh: 我想很多人来向你寻求关于转型的建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me just ask when folks come to you asking, "Should I stick with my idea?
zh: 就让我问问，当人们来问你，“我应该坚持我的想法吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Should I pivot?"
zh: 我应该转型吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: what sort of advice do you find most helps them?
zh: 你发现什么样的建议最能帮助他们？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I mean, I think it's partly an intuition because obviously the decision is about, "Have you exhausted the possibilities?"
zh: 是的，我想这部分是直觉，因为显然这个决定是关于，“你是否已经穷尽了各种可能性？”

speaker: Unknown
en: and in the case where we were working on Glitch, this game where we used IRC for internal communication and we added a bunch of IRC which became the Proto Slack.
zh: 在我们做 Glitch 的情况下，这个游戏我们用 IRC 进行内部沟通，我们添加了一堆 IRC 这成为了 Proto Slack。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think Slack had an enormous advantage in the fact that we are working on this for several years without actually explicitly working on it and only doing the minimum number of features that were absolutely guaranteed to be successful in the sense that it was so irritating that we couldn't stand it anymore or such an obvious improvement that we couldn't help but take advantage of it.
zh: 我认为 Slack 有一个巨大的优势，事实上我们在没有明确地做这件事的情况下为此工作了几年，只做绝对保证成功的最小数量的功能，就其意义而言，它是如此令人恼火以至于我们再也无法忍受了，或者是如此明显的改进以至于我们忍不住利用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: We still had $ 9 million left and everyone still liked the game and we were all happy working on it, but I think by that point I had exhausted every non-verdiculous long shot idea to make it commercially successful, and so I decided to abandon it.
zh: 我们还剩下 900 万美元，每个人都仍然喜欢这个游戏，我们都很高兴做它，但我想在那时我已经穷尽了所有非荒谬的长期想法来使其商业成功，所以我决定放弃它。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the default advice for anyone in anything is persevere.
zh: 但对任何人在任何事情上的默认建议是坚持。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like a kitten hanging off the branch and a poster says, "Hang in there."
zh: 就像一只小猫挂在树枝上，海报上写着“坚持下去”。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's so many stories of, "So-and-so started out going door-to-door and was rejected by everyone and then suddenly there was Nike," or something like that and just, "If you stick with it long enough, you'll eventually be successful."
zh: 有很多这样的故事，“某某人开始时挨家挨户推销，被所有人拒绝，然后突然有了 Nike，”或类似的事情，只是，“如果你坚持足够长的时间，你最终会成功。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I think you have to really be coldly rational.
zh: 我认为你必须真正保持冷酷的理性。

speaker: Unknown
en: Some of this shows up in the book Thinking in Bets.
zh: 其中一些出现在《对赌》这本书中。

speaker: Unknown
en: Some of it's in Annie Duke's second book, the title of which I'm forgetting right now, but someone will know it.
zh: 其中一些在 Annie Duke 的第二本书中，我现在忘记了标题，但有人会知道的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, Thinking in Bets, and then what was the second?
zh: 是的，《对赌》，然后第二本是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I forget.
zh: 我忘了。

speaker: Unknown
en: She actually uses Glitch and Slack as an example of a smart fold basically.
zh: 她实际上以 Glitch 和 Slack 为例，把它作为一个基本上聪明的案例。

speaker: Unknown
en: My expected value here has diminished to the point where this alternative looks more attractive.
zh: 我这边的期望价值已经下降到了这样一个地步，以至于这个替代方案看起来更有吸引力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the reason I say you have to be coldly rational about it is because it's fucking humiliating.
zh: 我说你必须对此保持冷酷的理性的原因是因为这真的很羞耻。

speaker: Unknown
en: I convinced so many and you have to convince so many people to get a company off the ground.
zh: 我说服了很多人，而且你也必须说服很多人才能让一家公司起步。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to go to investors.
zh: 你必须去见投资人。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to go to early employees and say, "You should leave your other job and come work for this because here's the incredible feature we're imagining."
zh: 你必须去见早期员工，并说，「你应该辞掉你的其他工作来这里为这个工作，因为我们正在设想这个不可思议的功能。」

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to go to the press and you have to make all these promises and you have users and you've committed things to the users and you've convinced them to give up their time for this thing.
zh: 你必须去见媒体，你必须做出所有这些承诺，而且你有用户，你已经向用户承诺了一些事情，你已经说服他们为这件事付出他们的时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think for a lot of people, it feels better to just keep doing it until it dies of suffocation due to lack of capital or something like that.
zh: 所以我认为对很多人来说，继续做下去直到它因为缺乏资金或类似原因而窒息而亡，感觉会更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then just to admit, "Okay, I was wrong.
zh: 而不是直接承认，「好的，我错了。

speaker: Unknown
en: This didn't work," and it's humiliating.
zh: 这没成功，」而且这很羞耻。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's painful.
zh: 这很痛苦。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's wrenching.
zh: 这很折磨。

speaker: Unknown
en: It has a bad impact.
zh: 它有很糟糕的影响。

speaker: Unknown
en: When we shut down Glitch, there was a lot of people who loved it and would spend all of their free time and couldn't wait to get home from work to go play it more.
zh: 当我们关闭 Glitch 时，有很多人爱它，会花费他们所有的空闲时间，迫不及待地想下班回家去多玩一会儿。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was their community and the community just disappeared, all these people and all these identities that have been created.
zh: 那是他们的社区，而那个社区就这样消失了，所有这些人，以及所有已建立的身份。

speaker: Unknown
en: And obviously, people lost their jobs and people who had moved their families to a different city in order to take this job now weren't going to have a job anymore.
zh: 很明显，人们失去了工作，那些为了这份工作把家庭搬到另一个城市的人，现在不会再有工作了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So pivots aren't something I take lightly.
zh: 所以转向不是一件我会轻率对待的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's very different to be like, "There's three of us and we started making this app and then we pivoted to a different app."
zh: 我认为这很不一样，比如说，「我们要三个人，我们开始制作这个应用，然后我们转向了一个不同的应用。」

speaker: Unknown
en: That doesn't even really count.
zh: 那甚至真的不算数。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're six months into something, you're still messing around.
zh: 如果你做某件事才六个月，你还在瞎折腾。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're trying to figure out what it is that you're building.
zh: 你还在试图弄清楚你在建造的是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not really a pivot.
zh: 这真的不是一个转向。

speaker: Unknown
en: Obviously in this case, it worked out great and there's survivorship bias and that doesn't mean that everyone should pivot all the time.
zh: 显然在这个案例中，结果非常好，而且存在幸存者偏差，这并不意味着每个人都应该一直转向。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think creating the distance so that you can make an intellectual, rational decision about it rather than an emotional decision is essential.
zh: 但我认为创造这种距离，以便你能够对此做出一个理性的、理性的决定，而不是一个情绪化的决定，是必不可少的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love, also, your piece of advice of just exhaust.
zh: 我也喜欢你的这个建议，就是穷尽。

speaker: Unknown
en: Once you've exhausted all the ideas, that's a really good time to see what else is out there.
zh: 一旦你已经穷尽了所有的想法，那是去看看还有什么别的的好时机。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, just all the good ideas.
zh: 是的，就是所有的好主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: All the good ideas,
zh: 所有的好主意，

speaker: Unknown
en: All the realistic.
zh: 所有现实的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The point you made about just kind of persevering, I just had Melanie Perkins, CEO of Canva, in the podcast.
zh: 你刚才提到的关于只是坚持到底的观点，我刚才在播客里请到了 Canva 的 CEO Melanie Perkins。

speaker: Unknown
en: 100 investors rejected her before somebody finally decided to invest and she just kept pushing.
zh: 在终于有人决定投资之前，100 个投资人拒绝了她，而她一直在坚持。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that's a slightly different example, right?
zh: 我认为这是一个稍微不同的例子，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: She eventually believed in the concept of the product and in the vision.
zh: 她最终相信产品的概念和愿景。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was just trying to figure out the right articulation to get investors who ended up being obviously very, very happy.
zh: 只是试图找出正确的表达方式，以找到最终显然非常、非常开心的投资人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Extremely happy.
zh: 极其开心。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, geez.
zh: 噢，天哪。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe a final topic depending on how time goes.
zh: 也许取决于时间，最后一个话题。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to talk about generosity.
zh: 我想谈谈慷慨。

speaker: Unknown
en: I talked to a bunch of people, as I said, that have worked with you and the number one theme that came up again and again and again when I asked them about you and what has stuck most with them is just generosity.
zh: 正如我所说，我和一群和你共事过的人谈过，当我问起关于你和什么最让他们印象深刻时，再次出现的第一大主题就是慷慨。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I'm going to read a few examples that I heard from folks that are examples of your generosity over the years.
zh: 所以我要读几个我从人们那里听到的例子，这些是多年来你慷慨的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: So one person shared that he needed a little money before Christmas and he said, "Stewart literally walked me out of the building, went to the cash machine, handed me $500, told me to go home to my family."
zh: 所以一个人分享说他在圣诞节前需要一点钱，他说，「Stewart 实际上陪我走出了大楼，去了自动取款机，递给我 500 美元，告诉我回家陪家人。」

speaker: Unknown
en: Other folks shared that, when you talked about Glitch just recently when you had to lay people off, you cried real tears when you were laying people off and then you spent an incredible amount of time helping them find new jobs and extending their severance pay and just taking it extremely, extremely seriously, much more than I think most people feel like CEOs do.
zh: 其他人分享说，当你最近谈到 Glitch，当你不得不裁员时，你在裁员时流下了真实的眼泪，然后你花费了不可思议的大量时间帮助他们寻找新工作并延长他们的遣散费，而且对待这件事极其、极其认真，比我认为大多数人觉得 CEO 会做的好得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: Someone else shared that you paid 100% of employees health insurance to give them just fewer things to think about.
zh: 另一个人分享说你支付了 100% 的员工医疗保险，就是为了让他们少想点事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you went public, you basically created the best possible situation for employees, no lockup, direct listing.
zh: 当你上市时，你基本上为员工创造了尽可能好的情况，没有禁售期，直接上市。

speaker: Unknown
en: Also, with the structure of the Slack deal, people said that acquisition was very employee friendly.
zh: 此外，通过 Slack 交易的结构，人们说那次收购对员工非常友好。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's employees.
zh: 那是关于员工的。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's also just the way you thought about customers.
zh: 还有你考虑客户的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: A few examples: You gave free credits to businesses who were struggling to pay the bills during COVID.
zh: 举几个例子：你给那些在 COVID 期间难以支付账单的企业提供了免费额度。

speaker: Unknown
en: You released this fair billing, which I think was very innovative at the time, where you stopped charging people for seats they weren't using, even though they signed a deal to charge for those seats.
zh: 你发布了这个公平计费，我觉得这在当时非常有创新性，你停止向人们收取他们未使用的席位费用，即使他们签署了要为那些席位付费的协议。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of times, you slipped release schedules because you just wanted to make features better and better for people.
zh: 很多时候，你推迟了发布计划，因为你只想为人们把功能做得越来越好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'll end with this quote: "Stewart is a leader who takes the responsibility he feels for his employees personally, and to which he extends the most generous circumstances he could muster.
zh: 我最后用这句引言来结束：“Stewart 是一位领导者，他把对员工的责任看得很重，并尽其所能地为他们提供最慷慨的待遇。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That feels worth celebrating."
zh: 这感觉值得庆祝。

speaker: Unknown
en: So first of all, I just want to celebrate you.
zh: 所以首先，我只是想庆祝你。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's really rare and inspiring to meet a leader like that.
zh: 我认为遇见这样的领导者非常罕见且令人鼓舞。

speaker: Unknown
en: Clearly, you've had a lot of impact on a lot of people.
zh: 显然，你对很多人产生了很大的影响。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know exactly the question I want to ask, but I guess in what part is this intentional, just like, "This is how we win.
zh: 我不知道确切想问什么问题，但我想问这在多大程度上是有意的，就像，“这就是我们赢的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to be very generous and help people because I know this will help long-term"?
zh: 我会非常慷慨并帮助人们，因为我知道这会有助于长期发展”？

speaker: Unknown
en: How much of this is just a [inaudible 01:20:48] and it's just the way you are as a person?
zh: 这在多大程度上只是一种 [inaudible 01:20:48]，这仅仅是作为一个人你的行事方式？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think a lot of it is just the way I am as a person and I had wonderful parents who raised me right, but I think there is a little bit of a lesson there and I'm just going to assume people's familiarity with the prisoner's dilemma.
zh: 我认为其中大部分只是我作为一个人行事的方式，我有很好的父母，他们把我教育得很正，但我认为其中确实有一点教训，我就假设大家对囚徒困境都很熟悉。

speaker: Unknown
en: The acts of generosity to me are, "Oh, I am demonstrating that I am going to cooperate as we iterate in this game."
zh: 对我来说，慷慨的行为是，“噢，我正在表明随着我们在这个博弈中迭代，我将会合作。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you do that, then people will also cooperate and you both benefit.
zh: 如果你这样做，那么人们也会合作，你们双方都会受益。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whereas if you never really know if the other person is going to defect at the first opportunity, then your best bet is to defect.
zh: 而如果你永远不知道对方是否会在第一个机会就背叛，那么你最好的选择就是背叛。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there's a game theoretic aspect, usually in games that are much, much, much more complicated than the prisoner's dilemma.
zh: 所以这有一个博弈论的方面，通常是在比囚徒困境复杂得多的博弈中。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think one thing I didn't touch on before, but to me was important enough, is that at more than one company all hands, I made everyone in the company repeat this as a chant.
zh: 我想我之前没有提到的一件事，但对我来说非常重要的一点是，在不止一次的公司全员大会上，我让公司里的每个人都重复这句话作为口号。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was, "In the long run, the measure of our success will be the amount of value that we create for customers."
zh: 这句话是，“从长远来看，衡量我们成功的标准将是我们为客户创造的价值量。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I wanted to be super clear and explicit about that because it should be if anything you're doing feels like a little bit shady, a little bit cheating, a little bit maximizing at the wrong moment or taking advantage of a customer or anything like that, definitely shouldn't do it.
zh: 我想要对此非常清楚和明确，因为如果你做的任何事情感觉有点不正当，有点作弊，有点在错误的时刻最大化利益，或者利用客户或类似的事情，绝对不应该那样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because to me, I mean I think it's literally true, but it's also an ethical way to run a business.
zh: 因为对我来说，我认为这在字面上是真的，但这也是一种经营企业的道德方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's not just that the ethics are good.
zh: 不仅仅是因为道德是好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like there's advantages for you.
zh: 而是说这对你有好处。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're able to attract a better class of employees.
zh: 你能够吸引更高素质的员工。

speaker: Unknown
en: If all your employees are ethical, then it's going to be a better place for everyone to work and you're going to be happier and you're going to have fewer internal problems and all that stuff.
zh: 如果你所有的员工都是道德的，那么这对每个人来说都是一个更好的工作场所，你会更快乐，你会更少的内部问题等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think it really is true that especially in the long run, you can't destroy value for your customers and expect to be successful.
zh: 但我认为确实如此，特别是在长远来看，你不能摧毁客户的价值并期望获得成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to actually make their lives better.
zh: 你必须真正让他们的生活变得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you could put effort into pointing it out to them and demonstrating that you have created this value and stuff like that, but there's no substitute for actually having created it.
zh: 你可以努力向他们指出来并证明你创造了这种价值，但没有什么能替代真正创造了价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that is incredibly important and that implies a real generosity, whether that's in negotiating terms with an enterprise deal or that's policy decisions.
zh: 我认为这非常重要，这意味着真正的慷慨，无论这是在与企业交易谈判条款还是在政策决策中。

speaker: Unknown
en: One time that it blew up in our face was our SLA was like, "For any downtime, you get 100 times your money back."
zh: 有一次这给了我们惨痛的教训，我们的 SLA 是这样的，“对于任何停机时间，你会获得 100 倍的退款。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Because from my perspective, it's like if we're down for two minutes, it's like pennies.
zh: 因为从我的角度来看，就像如果我们停机两分钟，那就是几分钱。

speaker: Unknown
en: It doesn't really make any difference.
zh: 没什么大不了的。

speaker: Unknown
en: If we're down for 10 hours or something like that, then we have bigger problems than paying back people.
zh: 如果我们停机 10 小时或类似的，那么我们就有比向人们退款更大的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Fast-forward, we now have hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue and we've gone public.
zh: 快进到现在，我们有数亿美元的收入并且我们已经上市了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And shortly after we go public, we have one of the biggest outages we ever had.
zh: 在我们上市后不久，我们经历了有史以来最大的停机之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't remember how long it was, but it was many hours.
zh: 我不记得持续了多久，但有好几个小时。

speaker: Unknown
en: But by the time we got that scale, 100 times the money back for the third of a day that we were down was $8 million or something like that.
zh: 但是当我们达到那个规模时，对于停机的三分之一天，100 倍的退款是 800 万美元或类似的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: It didn't cost us any money because we just gave it to people in the form of credits, but it meant that a bunch of revenue that we had already anticipated for the next quarter wasn't going to show up because people's credits were going to offset what they would've otherwise paid us.
zh: 这并没有花我们任何钱，因为我们只是以额度的形式把它给了人们，但这意味着我们为下一个季度已经预期的一大笔收入不会出现了，因为人们的额度会抵消他们原本要付给我们的钱。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we definitely changed the terms of service after that because being a public company is a little bit different.
zh: 所以我们在那之后肯定更改了服务条款，因为作为一家上市公司有点不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: But in every other respect, I think they were all really important decisions that were helpful in us becoming successful.
zh: 但在其他所有方面，我认为它们都是真正重要的决定，有助于我们获得成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: Was that policy ... It was automatic?
zh: 那个政策... 是自动的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: You didn't even have to claim it.
zh: 你甚至都不需要去申请。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was just automatically you get this credit?
zh: 只是自动地你就得到了这个额度？

speaker: Unknown
en: And the default is you don't have to pay if you let us know.
zh: 默认是你不需要支付如果你告诉我们的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: This was, "We will automatically, proactively, preemptively without any input from you ..."
zh: 这是，“我们将自动地、主动地、先发制人地，不需要任何你的输入……”

speaker: Unknown
en: Too generous.
zh: 太慷慨了。

speaker: Unknown
en: "Apply this credit to your account, and just send you a message that it happened.
zh: “把这个额度应用到你的账户，只是发一条消息告诉你这发生了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the way, we will do it on the aggregate for downtime, even if the issue didn't affect you as a customer."
zh: 顺便说一下，我们将在停机的总时长基础上这样做，即使这个问题作为一个客户并没有影响到你。”

speaker: Lenny
en: Wow. Too generous. You found the edge of where you want to be.
zh: 哇。太慷慨了。你找到了你想要处于的边缘。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What was that mantra again that you had the company chant?
zh: 你让公司员工反复念叨的那句口头禅是什么来着？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think this is a really nice way to end it.
zh: 我觉得这是一个非常好的结尾方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was, "In the long run, the measure of our success will be the amount of value we create for customers."
zh: 是这样的，“从长远来看，衡量我们成功的标准将是我们为客户创造的价值量。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Incredible.
zh: 太不可思议了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm just trying to picture the entire team at Slack reciting this mantra.
zh: 我只是在想象 Slack 的整个团队齐声背诵这句口头禅的样子。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was hundreds of people.
zh: 有好几百人。

speaker: Unknown
en: It felt very like, Kim Jong-Un or Stalin or something like that.
zh: 感觉非常像金正恩或斯大林之类的样子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, on that note, most people don't know this about you, but your actual name when you were born was not Stewart.
zh: 好吧，说到这个，大多数人都不了解你的这一点，但你出生时的真名并不是 Stewart。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was Dharma.
zh: 是 Dharma。

speaker: Stewart Butterfield
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this all makes sense as you learn that.
zh: 当你知道这一点后，这一切都说得通了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: My name is Dharma Jeremy Butterfield, so my parents named me.
zh: 我的名字是 Dharma Jeremy Butterfield，这是我父母给我取的名字。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when I was 12, I changed it because I just really wanted to be normal and for some reason I thought Stewart was a normal name.
zh: 我在 12 岁时改了名，因为我当时真的很想变得正常，出于某种原因我认为 Stewart 是一个普通的名字。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the way, you'll notice this now that I said it.
zh: 顺便说一句，既然我现在提到了，你就会注意到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Any character except for Stuart Little the mouse, anytime you see a character in a movie, a novel, TV show or whatever, there's only the loser Stewart and the asshole Stewart.
zh: 除了老鼠 Stuart Little 之外，任何时候你在电影、小说、电视剧或其他什么作品中看到一个角色，只有失败者 Stewart 和混蛋 Stewart 这两种。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's obviously, in the collective consciousness, a terrible name and I shouldn't have chosen it and I regret it.
zh: 显然，在集体意识中，这是一个糟糕的名字，我不应该选择它，我很后悔。

speaker: Unknown
en: But by the time I realized that, Dharma and Greg had already come out and it would've seemed like I was bandwagon jumping.
zh: 但当我意识到这一点时，电视剧《Dharma and Greg》已经播出了，这会看起来像我在跟风。

speaker: Unknown
en: And people thought it was a girl's name, even though in India it's obviously only a boy's name.
zh: 而且人们认为这是一个女孩的名字，尽管在印度这显然是一个男孩的名字。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to add just one last little tidbit because I forgot about this earlier on and I think it helps tie things together, and it's called the owner's delusion.
zh: 我想补充最后一个小插曲，因为我之前忘了提，而且我认为这有助于把事情联系起来，这叫做“所有者的错觉”。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this is based on something I posted on Twitter.
zh: 这是基于我在 Twitter 上发布的内容。

speaker: Unknown
en: The person who came up with the name later deleted their account and so I have no idea who it was and who to credit for this.
zh: 想出这个名字的人后来删除了他们的账户，所以我不知道那是谁，也不知道该归功于谁。

speaker: Unknown
en: But what I had posted was, and this was a long time ago when restaurant websites have gotten better and it doesn't really matter because Google Local was taking over everything, but this is, let's say, 10 years ago.
zh: 但我发布的是，那是在很久以前，当时餐厅的网站已经变得更好了，但这并不重要，因为 Google Local 正在接管一切，但这大约是在 10 年前。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's five things you could possibly want when you go to a restaurant's website and it's their street address, their phone number, the menu, the hours of operation ... Oh my God, I'm forgetting the fifth thing.
zh: 当你去餐厅的网站时，你可能想要五样东西，那就是它们的街道地址、电话号码、菜单、营业时间……天哪，我忘了第五样东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, and making a reservation, how to make a reservation.
zh: 哦，还有预订，如何预订。

speaker: Unknown
en: And again, this problem has to some extent taken care of it's itself or at least improved, but what you would get was this super slow loading photo, the Ken Burns effect as it [inaudible 01:27:30] ...
zh: 同样，这个问题在某种程度上已经自己解决了，或者至少有所改善，但你会得到的是一张加载极慢的照片，Ken Burns 特效效果当它[听不清 01:27:30]时……

speaker: Unknown
en: The flashed.
zh: 闪现。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then fading in and then some music starts playing.
zh: 然后淡入，然后开始播放一些音乐。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then if they show you the phone number, it's not clickable.
zh: 然后，如果他们给你看电话号码，它是不可点击的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Image.
zh: 图片。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not even text that you can copy because yes, it's an image.
zh: 它甚至不是你可以复制的文本，是的，它是一张图片。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they don't have the hours.
zh: 而且他们没有营业时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't put the address or whatever and it's just like, "What?"
zh: 他们不放地址或其他什么东西，就像，“什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: For sure, whoever made this website for the restaurant owner and the restaurant owner themselves have definitely been in the position where they went to somebody else's restaurant website because they wanted to get the address or the opening hours or the phone number or whatever.
zh: 肯定，无论谁为餐厅主制作了这个网站，以及餐厅主本人，肯定都遇到过这样的处境：他们去过别人的餐厅网站，因为他们想要获取地址或营业时间或电话号码或其他什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: So why does it end up like this and what should we call this?
zh: 那么为什么最终会变成这样，我们应该称之为什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And whoever replied to the tweet, she said, "We should call it the owner's delusion," and I was like, "Oh my God.
zh: 无论谁回复了那条推文，她说，“我们应该称之为‘所有者的错觉’”，我就想，“天哪。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's perfect."
zh: 这太完美了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that is incredibly powerful and what ends up with the result, like Apple naming whatever that feature is called Sleep, which it's too hard to understand what that can possibly mean.
zh: 我认为这是非常有力的，最终导致了像 Apple 把那个功能命名为 Sleep 这样的结果，这太难理解那可能意味着什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's why people anticipate, despite the fact that when they get to your website for the first time, their intent is absolutely the minimum number of micro points above the threshold required from them to actually take that action.
zh: 这就是为什么人们会预期，尽管当他们第一次访问你的网站时，他们的意图绝对只是略高于实际采取该行动所需的最低阈值的微积分分。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're like, "All right.
zh: 你就像，“好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Welcome to my website," and there's a bunch of BS and there's a bunch of stuff that doesn't make any sense and the buttons are inscrutable.
zh: 欢迎来到我的网站”，然后有一堆废话，有一堆根本说不通的东西，按钮难以理解。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's unclear what to do next because I think that my thing is so important and I don't recognize that you are at work and you were late this morning and you have to go to the bathroom and you're just a regular human being who has stuff going on, that you're concerned that your kid is a fuck-up and they're getting in trouble at school and stuff like that.
zh: 而且不清楚下一步该做什么，因为我认为我的东西是如此重要，而没有意识到你在工作，你今天早上迟到了，你要去洗手间，你只是一个有事情要处理的普通人，你担心你的孩子是个废物，他们在学校惹麻烦之类的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're not subjects who paid money to go to your play and are sitting in the audience and waiting for that curtain to go out.
zh: 他们不是花钱买票去看你的戏并坐在观众席上等待大幕拉开的观众。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're people who are going to bounce in a fraction of a second.
zh: 他们是会在几分之一秒内跳走的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so everyone should always be conscious of the owner's solution.
zh: 所以每个人都应该始终意识到所有者的解决方案。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that.
zh: 我喜欢这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's the solution?
zh: 解决方案是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it have other people look at it and give you feedback?
zh: 是不是让别人看看它并给你反馈？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, and recognize it.
zh: 是的，并且要意识到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And unfortunately, it's one of those things like Murphy's Law.
zh: 不幸的是，这就像墨菲定律一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
